1	\chapter{Flat base change for the pushforward of a quasi-coherent sheaf ($i=0$)}
2	\label{chap:Cohomology_FlatBaseChange}
3	% SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Section "Cohomology and
4	% base change, I", and Tag 02KH (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex and
5	% references/stacks-coherent.md).
6	
7	\section{Setup and motivation}
8	
9	This chapter establishes the $i=0$ (i.e.\ \(H^0\) / direct-image) case of flat
10	base change: the formation of the pushforward \(f_*\mathcal{F}\) of a
11	quasi-coherent sheaf commutes with flat base change on the target. It is the
12	foundational, fully self-contained slice of the cohomology-and-base-change
13	package that ultimately underlies relative representability statements; the
14	higher derived cases \(R^i f_*\) for \(i \geq 1\) are treated separately and are
15	out of scope here.
16	
17	Throughout, \(f : X \to S\) is a morphism of schemes that is quasi-compact and
18	quasi-separated, and \(\mathcal{F}\) is a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module.
19	A base change of \(f\) along an arbitrary morphism \(g : S' \to S\) is recorded
20	by the cartesian square obtained from the fibre product
21	\(X' = X \times_S S'\):
22	\[
23	  \begin{array}{ccc}
24	    X' & \xrightarrow{\;g'\;} & X \\[2pt]
25	    {\scriptstyle f'}\big\downarrow & & \big\downarrow{\scriptstyle f} \\[2pt]
26	    S' & \xrightarrow{\;g\;} & S
27	  \end{array}
28	\]
29	Here \(f' : X' \to S'\) is the base change of \(f\), and \(g' : X' \to X\) is the
30	first projection. We write \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\) for the pullback
31	of \(\mathcal{F}\) to \(X'\).
32	
33	\section{The canonical base-change map}
34	
35	\begin{definition}\leanok
36	[Base-change map for the pushforward]
37	  \label{def:pushforward_base_change_map}
38	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushforwardBaseChangeMap}
39	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Section "Cohomology and
40	  % base change, I", base-change diagram (\ref{equation-base-change-diagram})
41	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L877--904).
42	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $f : X \to S$ be a morphism of schemes.
43	  % Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a quasi-coherent sheaf on $X$.
44	  % Suppose further that $g : S' \to S$ is any morphism of schemes. Denote
45	  % $X' = X_{S'} = S' \times_S X$ the base change of $X$ and denote
46	  % $f' : X' \to S'$ the base change of $f$.
47	  % Also write $g' : X' \to X$ the projection,
48	  % and set $\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}$."
49	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, \S\,Cohomology and base
50	  change, I.}
51	  Let \(f : X \to S\) be a morphism of schemes, \(\mathcal{F}\) a quasi-coherent
52	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module, and \(g : S' \to S\) an arbitrary morphism, giving the
53	  cartesian square above with projection \(g' : X' \to X\) and
54	  \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\). The \emph{base-change map for the
55	  pushforward} is the canonical morphism of \(\mathcal{O}_{S'}\)-modules
56	  \[
57	    g^*\bigl(f_*\mathcal{F}\bigr) \longrightarrow f'_*\bigl((g')^*\mathcal{F}\bigr)
58	    = f'_*\mathcal{F}' .
59	  \]
60	  It is adjoint to the morphism
61	  \(f_*\mathcal{F} \to g_*\, f'_*\mathcal{F}' = f_*\,(g')_*\mathcal{F}'\) obtained
62	  by applying \(f_*\) to the unit
63	  \(\mathcal{F} \to (g')_*(g')^*\mathcal{F}\) of the
64	  \(((g')^*, (g')_*)\)-adjunction, using the commutativity
65	  \(g \circ f' = f \circ g'\) of the square.
66	\end{definition}
67	
68	\section{The affine case}
69	
70	\subsection{Locality of isomorphisms for \texttt{Scheme.Modules} morphisms}
71	
72	Before treating the affine computation we record three project-local supplements
73	to Mathlib. Mathlib provides the per-open criterion
74	(\(\mathrm{IsIso}\,\varphi \iff \forall U,\ \mathrm{IsIso}(\varphi_U)\)) and a
75	stalkwise isomorphism criterion for \(\mathrm{TopCat.Sheaf}\)-valued morphisms, but
76	it does not package the stalk-local or basis-local criterion at the level of
77	morphisms of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. The following lemmas bridge that gap; they
78	are exactly the locality tools used in the first reduction of the affine
79	base-change lemma below, where one checks the base-change map after restricting to
80	the affine opens of the base. As bespoke infrastructure they carry no external
81	citation and stand on their proof sketches.
82	
83	\begin{lemma}\leanok
84	[Stalk-local criterion for isomorphisms of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules]
85	  \label{lem:modules_isIso_iff_stalk}
86	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Modules.isIso_iff_isIso_stalkFunctor_map}
87	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(\varphi : M \to N\) a morphism of sheaves of
88	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. Then \(\varphi\) is an isomorphism if and only if the
89	  underlying morphism of abelian-group presheaves induces an isomorphism on the
90	  stalk at every point \(x \in X\).
91	\end{lemma}
92	
93	\begin{proof}
94	
95	  If \(\varphi\) is an isomorphism, then so is its image under the forgetful
96	  functor to abelian-group presheaves, and the stalk functor at each \(x\),
97	  being a functor, carries isomorphisms to isomorphisms. Conversely, assume the
98	  stalk map is an isomorphism at every \(x\). Package the underlying abelian-group
99	  presheaves of \(M\) and \(N\) as sheaves of abelian groups, and \(\varphi\) as a
100	  morphism of these sheaves; the stalkwise-isomorphism hypothesis lets us apply the
101	  \(\mathrm{TopCat.Sheaf}\)-level stalk-local isomorphism criterion to conclude that
102	  this morphism of sheaves of abelian groups is an isomorphism. Pushing it through
103	  the forgetful functor to presheaves yields an isomorphism of the underlying
104	  abelian-group presheaf morphisms, and since the forgetful functor from
105	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules to abelian-group presheaves reflects isomorphisms,
106	  \(\varphi\) itself is an isomorphism.
107	\end{proof}
108	
109	\begin{lemma}\leanok
110	[Basis-local criterion for isomorphisms of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules]
111	  \label{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis}
112	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Modules.isIso_of_isIso_app_of_isBasis}
113	  \uses{lem:modules_isIso_iff_stalk}
114	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, let \(\{B_i\}_{i \in \iota}\) be a family of opens whose
115	  range is a basis of the topology of \(X\), and let \(\varphi : M \to N\) be a
116	  morphism of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. If \(\varphi\) restricts to an
117	  isomorphism on the sections over every basic open \(B_i\), then \(\varphi\) is an
118	  isomorphism.
119	\end{lemma}
120	
121	\begin{proof}
122	  \uses{lem:modules_isIso_iff_stalk}
123	  By Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_iff_stalk} it suffices to prove that the induced
124	  stalk map is bijective at each point \(x \in X\). For injectivity, a germ that
125	  vanishes at \(x\) already vanishes on some open neighbourhood, which may be
126	  shrunk to a basic open \(B_i\); on \(B_i\) the section map \(\varphi_{B_i}\) is
127	  injective, so the original section is zero, giving injectivity of the stalk map
128	  from injectivity over the basis. For surjectivity, an arbitrary germ at \(x\) is
129	  represented by a section of \(N\) over some basic open \(B_i\) containing \(x\);
130	  since \(\varphi_{B_i}\) is surjective, this section lifts to a section of \(M\)
131	  over \(B_i\), whose germ at \(x\) maps to the given germ. Thus the stalk map is
132	  bijective, and \(\varphi\) is an isomorphism.
133	\end{proof}
134	
135	\begin{lemma}\leanok
136	[Affine-open locality criterion for isomorphisms of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules]
137	  \label{lem:modules_isIso_iff_affineOpens}
138	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Modules.isIso_iff_isIso_app_affineOpens}
139	  \uses{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis}
140	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(\varphi : M \to N\) a morphism of sheaves of
141	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. Then \(\varphi\) is an isomorphism if and only if it
142	  restricts to an isomorphism on the sections over every affine open of \(X\).
143	\end{lemma}
144	
145	\begin{proof}
146	  \uses{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis}
147	  The forward implication is immediate, since the section functor over a fixed open
148	  preserves isomorphisms. For the converse, the affine opens of a scheme form a
149	  basis of its topology, so the claim is the special case of
150	  Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis} in which the basis is the family of
151	  affine opens.
152	\end{proof}
153	
154	\subsection{The affine computation}
155	
156	The whole result rests on the following elementary computation in the affine
157	setting, where the base-change map is visibly an isomorphism because tensoring is
158	associative. Before stating it we isolate the one ingredient not yet available as
159	a ready-made result on which the affine reduction turns: the description of the
160	pushforward of a tilde-module along a morphism of affine schemes.
161	
162	We first record three bespoke \(\Gamma\)-fragment supports that compute the global
163	sections of an affine pushforward; they carry no external citation and stand on
164	their proof sketches.
165	
166	\begin{lemma}
167	
168	\leanok
169	[Naturality of the global-sections comparison square]
170	  \label{lem:globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}
171	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}
172	  Let \(\varphi : R \to R'\) be a homomorphism of commutative rings, and write
173	  \(\mathrm{gs}_R : \Gamma(\operatorname{Spec} R, \top) \cong R\) and
174	  \(\mathrm{gs}_{R'} : \Gamma(\operatorname{Spec} R', \top) \cong R'\) for the
175	  global-sections isomorphisms of the structure sheaves. Then the ring square
176	  \[
177	    \mathrm{gs}_R \;\circ\; (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{\sharp}_{\top}
178	    \;=\; \varphi \;\circ\; \mathrm{gs}_{R'}
179	  \]
180	  commutes, where \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{\sharp}_{\top}\) is the
181	  top-open component of the structure-sheaf comparison map underlying
182	  \(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\).
183	\end{lemma}
184	
185	\begin{proof}
186	
187	  This is the naturality of the unit/counit of the
188	  \(\Gamma \dashv \operatorname{Spec}\) adjunction read off at the top open: the
189	  global-sections map of \(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\) is, under the
190	  global-sections isomorphisms on each side, conjugate to \(\varphi\) itself.
191	  Transposing the naturality square of the \(\operatorname{Spec}\) unit gives
192	  exactly the asserted ring equation.
193	\end{proof}
194	
195	\begin{lemma}
196	
197	\leanok
198	[Global sections of an affine pushforward]
199	  \label{lem:gammaPushforwardIso}
200	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gammaPushforwardIso}
201	  \uses{lem:globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}
202	  Let \(\varphi : R \to R'\) be a homomorphism of commutative rings and let
203	  \(N\) be an \(\operatorname{Spec}(R')\)-module. Then there is a canonical
204	  isomorphism of \(R\)-modules
205	  \[
206	    \Gamma\bigl((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_* N\bigr)
207	    \;\cong\;
208	    \operatorname{restr}_\varphi\bigl(\Gamma N\bigr),
209	  \]
210	  identifying the \(R\)-module of global sections of the pushforward with the
211	  restriction of scalars along \(\varphi\) of the \(R'\)-module of global sections
212	  of \(N\).
213	\end{lemma}
214	
215	\begin{proof}
216	  \uses{lem:globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}
217	  Because \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}(\top) = \top\), the global sections of
218	  the pushforward \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_* N\) are, on underlying abelian
219	  groups, the global sections of \(N\) with no transport along the open-set
220	  identification. The two sides therefore carry the same underlying abelian group;
221	  both unwind to nested restriction-of-scalars towers along the structure-sheaf
222	  global-sections maps. These towers are reconciled by applying the
223	  restriction-of-scalars composition identity twice and substituting the ring
224	  equation of
225	  Lemma~\ref{lem:globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}, which shows the
226	  resulting \(R\)-action is exactly restriction of scalars along \(\varphi\).
227	\end{proof}
228	
229	\begin{lemma}
230	
231	\leanok
232	[Global sections of an affine pushforward of a tilde-module]
233	  \label{lem:gammaPushforwardTildeIso}
234	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gammaPushforwardTildeIso}
235	  \uses{lem:gammaPushforwardIso}
236	  Let \(\varphi : R \to R'\) be a homomorphism of commutative rings and let \(M\)
237	  be an \(R'\)-module. Then there is a canonical isomorphism of \(R\)-modules
238	  \[
239	    \Gamma\bigl((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_* \widetilde{M}\bigr)
240	    \;\cong\;
241	    \operatorname{restr}_\varphi M .
242	  \]
243	\end{lemma}
244	
245	\begin{proof}
246	  \uses{lem:gammaPushforwardIso}
247	  Specialise Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIso} to \(N = \widetilde{M}\) and
248	  compose with the tilde-unit isomorphism
249	  \(\Gamma(\widetilde{M}) \cong M\) of \(R'\)-modules (which restricts to an
250	  isomorphism of \(R\)-modules after applying \(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi\)).
251	\end{proof}
252	
253	\begin{lemma}
254	[Sections of an affine pushforward over an arbitrary open]
255	  \label{lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt}
256	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gammaPushforwardIsoAt}
257	  \uses{lem:globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}
258	  Let \(\varphi : R \to R'\) be a homomorphism of commutative rings, let \(N\) be
259	  an \(\operatorname{Spec}(R')\)-module, and let \(U\) be an open of
260	  \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\), with preimage
261	  \(V = (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}(U)\) in \(\operatorname{Spec}(R')\). Then
262	  there is a canonical isomorphism of \(R\)-modules
263	  \[
264	    \Gamma\bigl((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_* N,\, U\bigr)
265	    \;\cong\;
266	    \operatorname{restr}_\varphi\bigl(\Gamma(N,\, V)\bigr) .
267	  \]
268	  This is the open-indexed generalization of
269	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIso}, which is the case \(U = \top\) (so that
270	  \(V = \top\) as well). The family of these isomorphisms is natural in the open
271	  argument: the structure-sheaf restriction maps on the two sides commute with the
272	  isomorphisms. For \(U = D(a)\) it is exactly the linear equivalence
273	  \(e_{D(a)}\) of movement~(1) in the proof of
274	  Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso}.
275	\end{lemma}
276	
277	\begin{proof}
278	  \uses{lem:globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}
279	  The construction copies that of Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIso} verbatim,
280	  with the top open \(\top\) replaced throughout by the arbitrary open \(U\) (and
281	  its preimage \(V\)). The point that makes this replacement free is that the
282	  \(R\)-module structure on the sections is supplied \emph{uniformly} by
283	  restriction of scalars along the global-sections ring map at the top open,
284	  independently of the evaluation open; consequently both sides unwind to the same
285	  nested restriction-of-scalars towers, reconciled by applying the
286	  restriction-of-scalars composition identity twice and substituting the
287	  \(\top\)-level ring equation of
288	  Lemma~\ref{lem:globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}. Naturality in the open
289	  follows because every constituent of the construction is either a structure-sheaf
290	  restriction map or conjugation by a fixed ring map, and all of these commute with
291	  further restriction along an inclusion of opens.
292	\end{proof}
293	
294	\begin{lemma}
295	[The tilde restriction from the top open to a basic open is a localization]
296	  \label{lem:tildeRestriction_isLocalizedModule}
297	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.tildeRestriction_isLocalizedModule}
298	  Let \(M\) be an \(R'\)-module and \(b \in R'\). Then the structure-sheaf
299	  restriction map
300	  \[
301	    \Gamma(\widetilde M,\, \top) \longrightarrow \Gamma(\widetilde M,\, D(b)),
302	  \]
303	  read as a map of \(R'\)-modules, exhibits \(\Gamma(\widetilde M, D(b))\) as the
304	  localization of \(\Gamma(\widetilde M, \top)\) at \(\mathrm{powers}(b)\). This is
305	  the \(R'\)-side localization fact transported in movement~(3) of the proof of
306	  Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso}.
307	\end{lemma}
308	
309	\begin{proof}
310	  By the defining property of \(\widetilde{(-)}\), the tilde-localization map
311	  \(M \to \Gamma(\widetilde M, D(b))\) exhibits its target as the localization of
312	  \(M\) at \(\mathrm{powers}(b)\). The global-sections map
313	  \(M \to \Gamma(\widetilde M, \top)\) is the localization of \(M\) at the trivial
314	  submonoid \(\mathrm{powers}(1)\) — since \(D(1) = \top\) — and is therefore
315	  bijective. The triangle identity relating these two localization maps to the
316	  structure-sheaf restriction \(\Gamma(\widetilde M, \top) \to
317	  \Gamma(\widetilde M, D(b))\) then exhibits the restriction itself, post-composed
318	  with the inverse of the bijection \(M \cong \Gamma(\widetilde M, \top)\), as the
319	  localization of \(\Gamma(\widetilde M, \top)\) at \(\mathrm{powers}(b)\).
320	\end{proof}
321	
322	We record three further bespoke supports that drive the basic-open verification of
323	the affine-pushforward isomorphism below. They carry no external citation and stand
324	on their proof sketches.
325	
326	\begin{lemma}
327	
328	\leanok
329	[Restriction of scalars of a localized module]
330	  \label{lem:powers_restrictScalars}
331	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsLocalizedModule.powers_restrictScalars}
332	  Let \(A\) be a commutative \(R\)-algebra, \(S \subseteq R\) a submonoid, and
333	  \(f : M \to N\) an \(A\)-linear map that exhibits \(N\) as the localization of
334	  \(M\) at the image submonoid
335	  \(\operatorname{algMap}_A(S) \subseteq A\). Then the underlying \(R\)-linear map
336	  \(\operatorname{restr}_R f\) exhibits \(N\) as the localization of \(M\) at \(S\)
337	  itself.
338	\end{lemma}
339	
340	\begin{proof}
341	
342	  This is the exact converse of the standard fact that a localization of \(M\) at a
343	  submonoid \(S\) of \(R\) remains a localization after enlarging the base to an
344	  \(R\)-algebra \(A\) along the image submonoid \(\operatorname{algMap}_A(S)\). One
345	  checks the three defining conditions of a localized module directly. Each
346	  \(s \in S\) acts invertibly on \(N\) because its image
347	  \(\operatorname{algMap}_A(s)\) does and the two actions coincide by the scalar
348	  tower \(R \to A \to N\). Surjectivity of \(f\) up to denominators and the
349	  equality-witness condition transport along the map
350	  \(s \mapsto \operatorname{algMap}_A(s)\) from \(S\) onto
351	  \(\operatorname{algMap}_A(S)\), again using
352	  \(\operatorname{algMap}_A(s)\cdot{} = s\cdot{}\). In the application
353	  \(A = R'\), \(S = \mathrm{powers}(a)\), one has
354	  \(\operatorname{algMap}_{R'}(\mathrm{powers}(a)) = \mathrm{powers}(\varphi a)\), so
355	  this identifies the localization of \(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\) at \(a\)
356	  with the localization of \(M\) at \(\varphi a\); it is the ring-change core of the
357	  basic-open computation below.
358	\end{proof}
359	
360	\begin{lemma}
361	
362	\leanok
363	[Section-level counit isomorphism from a localization hypothesis]
364	  \label{lem:fromTildeGamma_app_isIso_of_localized}
365	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.fromTildeΓ_app_isIso_of_isLocalizedModule}
366	  Let \(N\) be an \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\)-module and \(a \in R\). Suppose the
367	  structure-sheaf restriction map \(\Gamma(N, \top) \to \Gamma(N, D(a))\), read as
368	  an \(R\)-linear map of the global-section modules, exhibits \(\Gamma(N, D(a))\) as
369	  the localization of \(\Gamma(N, \top)\) at \(\mathrm{powers}(a)\). Then the
370	  tilde--\(\Gamma\) counit
371	  \(\mathrm{fromTilde}\Gamma\,N : \widetilde{\Gamma(N,\top)} \to N\) restricts to an
372	  isomorphism on the sections over the basic open \(D(a)\).
373	\end{lemma}
374	
375	\begin{proof}
376	
377	  Over \(D(a)\) the section map \(L\) of the counit sits in the triangle identity
378	  \(L \circ j = \rho\), where
379	  \(j : \Gamma(N,\top) \to \Gamma\bigl(\widetilde{\Gamma(N,\top)}, D(a)\bigr)\) is
380	  the tilde-localization map and \(\rho : \Gamma(N,\top) \to \Gamma(N, D(a))\) is
381	  the structure-sheaf restriction. Both \(j\) and \(\rho\) exhibit their targets as
382	  the localization of \(\Gamma(N,\top)\) at \(\mathrm{powers}(a)\) — \(j\) by the
383	  defining property of \(\widetilde{(-)}\), and \(\rho\) by hypothesis. By the
384	  uniqueness of localized modules there is a unique \(R\)-linear isomorphism \(e\)
385	  with \(e \circ j = \rho\); the triangle identity then forces \(L = e\), so \(L\) is
386	  an isomorphism. (This is the per-basic-open input feeding the basis-local
387	  criterion Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis}.)
388	\end{proof}
389	
390	\begin{lemma}
391	
392	\leanok
393	[Affine pushforward of a tilde-module, conditional form]
394	  \label{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso_conditional}
395	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushforward_spec_tilde_iso_of_isLocalizedModule}
396	  \uses{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis, lem:gammaPushforwardTildeIso,
397	  lem:fromTildeGamma_app_isIso_of_localized}
398	  Let \(\varphi : R \to R'\) be a homomorphism of commutative rings and \(M\) an
399	  \(R'\)-module. Suppose that for every \(a \in R\) the structure-sheaf restriction
400	  of the pushforward \(N := (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_*\widetilde M\) exhibits its
401	  sections over \(D(a)\) as the localization of its global sections at
402	  \(\mathrm{powers}(a)\). Then there is a canonical isomorphism of
403	  \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\)-modules
404	  \[
405	    (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_*\widetilde M \;\cong\;
406	    \widetilde{\bigl(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\bigr)} .
407	  \]
408	\end{lemma}
409	
410	\begin{proof}
411	  \uses{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis, lem:gammaPushforwardTildeIso,
412	  lem:fromTildeGamma_app_isIso_of_localized}
413	  Write \(N := (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_*\widetilde M\). Under the hypothesis,
414	  Lemma~\ref{lem:fromTildeGamma_app_isIso_of_localized} applies for each \(a \in R\),
415	  so the counit \(\mathrm{fromTilde}\Gamma\,N\) restricts to an isomorphism on the
416	  sections over every basic open \(D(a)\). Since the basic opens form a basis of
417	  \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\), the basis-local criterion
418	  Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis} upgrades this to: the counit
419	  \(\mathrm{fromTilde}\Gamma\,N\) is an isomorphism, i.e.\ \(N\) lies in the
420	  essential image of \(\widetilde{(-)}\). Composing the inverse counit with the
421	  tilde of the global-sections comparison
422	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardTildeIso} yields the asserted object isomorphism.
423	\end{proof}
424	
425	\begin{lemma}
426	% NOTE: The three route-iii supports now have their own pinned blocks above —
427	% `lem:powers_restrictScalars` (ring-change core), `lem:fromTildeGamma_app_isIso_of_localized`
428	% (section-level counit engine), and `lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso_conditional` (the assembly
429	% that takes the per-basic-open localization fact `hloc` as an explicit hypothesis). The
430	% unconditional decl named in the `\lean{}` pin below is the one remaining open obligation: it must
431	% supply `hloc` for `N := (Spec φ)_* M^~` and feed it to the conditional form. The sole difficulty
432	% in `hloc` is the structure-sheaf smul carrier wall over `D(a)`, whose element-free resolution is
433	% the `D(a)`-specialization of `lem:gammaPushforwardIso` spelled out in the proof below. Pin stays
434	% on the unconditional decl until the prover closes it.
435	[Affine pushforward of a tilde-module]
436	  \label{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso}
437	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushforward_spec_tilde_iso}
438	  Let \(\varphi : R \to R'\) be a homomorphism of commutative rings and let \(M\)
439	  be an \(R'\)-module. Then there is a canonical isomorphism of
440	  \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\)-modules
441	  \[
442	    \bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)_*\,\widetilde{M}
443	    \;\cong\;
444	    \widetilde{\bigl(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\bigr)},
445	  \]
446	  where \(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi : \operatorname{Spec}(R') \to
447	  \operatorname{Spec}(R)\) is the induced morphism of affine schemes,
448	  \(\widetilde{(-)}\) denotes the quasi-coherent sheaf associated to a module, and
449	  \(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\) is the \(R\)-module obtained from \(M\) by
450	  restriction of scalars along \(\varphi\). That is, pushing the quasi-coherent
451	  sheaf \(\widetilde{M}\) forward along \(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\) is, up to
452	  this canonical identification, the tilde of the restriction of scalars of \(M\).
453	
454	  This is the classical fact that the direct image of a quasi-coherent sheaf along
455	  a morphism of affine schemes is computed on modules by restriction of scalars; it
456	  is the affine companion of the pullback formula (the affine case of the
457	  pushforward analogue of Stacks ``$\widetilde{M}$ and pullback''). It is recorded
458	  here as bespoke project infrastructure because the affine-pushforward
459	  identification does not appear as a ready-made result in the existing
460	  quasi-coherent sheaf theory.
461	\end{lemma}
462	
463	\begin{proof}
464	  \uses{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis, lem:gammaPushforwardTildeIso,
465	  lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso_conditional, lem:powers_restrictScalars,
466	  lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt, lem:tildeRestriction_isLocalizedModule}
467	  We build the isomorphism \emph{directly on a basis of basic opens}. This is the
468	  non-circular route: checking the comparison map on basic opens yields the object
469	  isomorphism, and quasi-coherence of the pushforward is then a corollary, not a
470	  prerequisite. (One must not reverse this order: the global-sections comparison
471	  \(\Gamma\bigl((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_*\widetilde{M}\bigr) \cong
472	  \operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\) of
473	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardTildeIso} alone would only upgrade to an object
474	  isomorphism once the pushforward is already known to be quasi-coherent, so
475	  quasi-coherence cannot be deduced from an object isomorphism built that way.)
476	
477	  \emph{The comparison morphism.} There is a canonical morphism of
478	  \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\)-modules
479	  \[
480	    \alpha : \widetilde{\bigl(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\bigr)}
481	    \longrightarrow \bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)_*\widetilde{M},
482	  \]
483	  namely the tilde-adjoint of the global-sections identification of
484	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardTildeIso}. We show \(\alpha\) is an isomorphism.
485	
486	  \medskip
487	  \emph{Reduction to basic opens.} The basic opens \(D(a)\), for \(a \in R\), form a
488	  basis of the topology of \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\). By the basis-local criterion
489	  Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis} it suffices to check that \(\alpha\)
490	  restricts to an isomorphism on the sections over each basic open \(D(a)\).
491	
492	  \medskip
493	  \emph{The computation over \(D(a)\).} On the source, the sections of
494	  \(\widetilde{(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M)}\) over \(D(a)\) are the
495	  localization \(\bigl(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\bigr)[1/a]\) as an
496	  \(R[1/a]\)-module. On the target, since \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}D(a) =
497	  D(\varphi a)\), the sections of \(\bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)_*
498	  \widetilde{M}\) over \(D(a)\) are the sections of \(\widetilde{M}\) over
499	  \(D(\varphi a)\), i.e.\ the localization \(M[1/\varphi a]\), regarded as an
500	  \(R[1/a]\)-module by restriction of scalars along the induced map
501	  \(R[1/a] \to R'[1/\varphi a]\). These two localizations agree: the element \(a\)
502	  acts on \(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\) through \(\varphi a\), so localizing
503	  \(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\) at the multiplicative set generated by \(a\) is
504	  the same module as localizing \(M\) at the multiplicative set generated by
505	  \(\varphi a\). Both are the universal localization of
506	  \(M\) inverting \(\varphi a\), so the comparison map \(\alpha\) is an isomorphism
507	  on the sections over \(D(a)\). As this holds for every basic open, \(\alpha\) is
508	  an isomorphism.
509	
510	  \medskip
511	  \emph{The explicit formalization route over \(D(a)\).} The agreement of the two
512	  localizations just described is the entire content, but it must be realized
513	  through the global ring \(R\), because the \(R\)-action on the pushforward
514	  sections over \(D(a)\) is built from the global-sections module of
515	  \(N := (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_*\widetilde M\) rather than directly from
516	  \(R[1/a]\); a naive section-level identity \(a \cdot x = \varphi(a) \cdot x\) does
517	  not present itself. The reduction therefore proceeds exactly as the conditional
518	  assembly Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso_conditional} prescribes: it
519	  suffices to supply, for each \(a \in R\), the hypothesis \(\mathrm{hloc}(a)\) that
520	  the structure-sheaf restriction \(\Gamma(N, \top) \to \Gamma(N, D(a))\) exhibits
521	  \(\Gamma(N, D(a))\) as the localization of \(\Gamma(N, \top)\) at
522	  \(\mathrm{powers}(a)\). We construct \(\mathrm{hloc}(a)\) by transporting the
523	  already-known \(R'\)-side localization, in element-free fashion, along a
524	  \(D(a)\)-level analogue of the \(\top\)-level identification
525	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIso}. This proceeds in three movements.
526	
527	  \emph{(1) The \(D(a)\)-level linear equivalence.} In exact analogy to the
528	  \(\top\)-level Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIso}, construct an \(R\)-linear
529	  isomorphism
530	  \[
531	    e_{D(a)} : \Gamma\bigl((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_*\widetilde M,\, D(a)\bigr)
532	      \;\cong\;
533	      \operatorname{restr}_\varphi\bigl(\Gamma(\widetilde M,\, D(\varphi a))\bigr) .
534	  \]
535	  Its construction copies that of Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIso} verbatim — two
536	  reconciliations of nested restriction-of-scalars towers along the structure-sheaf
537	  section maps, glued by a canonical isomorphism from the ring equation — except that the
538	  canonical isomorphism now rests on a \emph{\(D(a)\)-level} ring equation in place of
539	  the \(\top\)-level one of
540	  Lemma~\ref{lem:globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}. The localization map
541	  \(R \to R[1/a]\) plays here the role that the global-section ring map plays at
542	  \(\top\).
543	
544	  \emph{(2) The \(D(a)\)-level ring equation.} The ring equation grounding this
545	  canonical isomorphism is the \(D(a)\)-shadow of
546	  Lemma~\ref{lem:globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}: writing
547	  \(g_R : R \to \Gamma(\operatorname{Spec} R, \top)\) and
548	  \(g_{R'} : R' \to \Gamma(\operatorname{Spec} R', \top)\) for the global-section
549	  ring maps and \(\mathrm{res}\) for the structure-sheaf restrictions,
550	  \[
551	    (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{\sharp}_{D(a)}
552	      \circ \mathrm{res}_{\top \to D(a)} \circ g_R
553	    \;=\;
554	    \mathrm{res}_{\top \to D(\varphi a)} \circ g_{R'} \circ \varphi ,
555	  \]
556	  using \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}D(a) = D(\varphi a)\), which is
557	  definitional. This is the sheaf-naturality of the structure-sheaf comparison map
558	  of \(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\) evaluated at \(D(a)\); it states that the
559	  \(\Gamma(\operatorname{Spec} R, \top)\)-action on the pushforward sections over
560	  \(D(a)\) factors, after restriction, through
561	  \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{\sharp}_{D(a)}\).
562	
563	  \emph{(3) Discharging \(\mathrm{hloc}(a)\) by transport.} The target
564	  \(\Gamma(\widetilde M, D(\varphi a)) = M[1/\varphi a]\) is canonically the
565	  \(\mathrm{powers}(\varphi a)\)-localization of \(M = \Gamma(\widetilde M, \top)\)
566	  over \(R'\). Apply the ring-change converse
567	  Lemma~\ref{lem:powers_restrictScalars} (with \(R\)-algebra \(A = R'\) and submonoid
568	  \(\mathrm{powers}(a)\), so that
569	  \(\operatorname{algMap}_{R'}(\mathrm{powers}(a)) = \mathrm{powers}(\varphi a)\)) to
570	  obtain the \(\mathrm{powers}(a)\)-localization over \(R\) of the same module,
571	  viewed by restriction of scalars. Transporting that localization property back
572	  across the linear equivalence \(e_{D(a)}\) of movement (1) — via the
573	  transport-along-an-equivalence principle for localized modules — yields
574	  the localization property at \(\mathrm{powers}(a)\) on
575	  \(\Gamma(N, D(a))\) over \(R\), i.e.\ \(\mathrm{hloc}(a)\). Feeding the family
576	  \(\{\mathrm{hloc}(a)\}_{a \in R}\) to the conditional assembly
577	  Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso_conditional} produces the unconditional
578	  object isomorphism \(\alpha\). The element-free \(\top\)-level template is
579	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIso}, and the \(D(a)\)-level construction above is
580	  its specialization with the localization map \(R \to R[1/a]\) in place of the
581	  global ring map.
582	
583	  \medskip
584	  \emph{Naturality in the open drives the transport.} The comparison assembled
585	  here, \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_* \circ \widetilde{(-)} \cong
586	  \widetilde{(-)} \circ \operatorname{restr}_\varphi\), is natural in \emph{both}
587	  arguments — in the module \(M\) and in the open \(U\). The naturality in the
588	  module is the usual functoriality; the naturality in the open is the substance of
589	  movement~(1): the family \(\{e_U\}_U\) of
590	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt} commutes with the structure-sheaf
591	  restriction maps on both sides. It is precisely this open-naturality that drives
592	  the localization transport \emph{uniformly}: the per-\(a\) localization fact
593	  \(\mathrm{hloc}(a)\) follows from the \(\top\)-level localization
594	  Lemma~\ref{lem:tildeRestriction_isLocalizedModule} together with the naturality
595	  square for the inclusion \(D(a) \hookrightarrow \top\), rather than from a
596	  section-level naturality square re-proved by hand for each individual \(a\).
597	
598	  The \(R\)-module structure on the \(R'\)-side sections to which the ring-change
599	  converse Lemma~\ref{lem:powers_restrictScalars} is applied is the honest
600	  restriction-of-scalars structure: \(R\) acts through \(\varphi\), i.e.\ via the
601	  \(R\)-algebra structure on \(R'\) determined by \(\varphi\) and the associated
602	  scalar tower \(R \to R' \to M[1/\varphi a]\). It is \emph{not} an ad-hoc,
603	  recursively-defined scalar action. Lemma~\ref{lem:powers_restrictScalars} is
604	  stated against exactly this restriction-of-scalars / scalar-tower structure, and
605	  it is under that structure that localizing \(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\) at
606	  \(a\) coincides with localizing \(M\) at \(\varphi a\).
607	
608	  % NOTE: This lemma is the affine case of the general fact that the pushforward of
609	  % a quasi-coherent sheaf along an affine morphism is quasi-coherent. In current
610	  % Mathlib master that fact is the IsLocalizing / fromTildeGamma characterization
611	  % (AlgebraicGeometry.isIso_fromTildeΓ_pushforward); it post-dates the project's
612	  % pinned Mathlib, so the project proves it in-tree by the basic-open route above.
613	  % The in-tree proof remains valid and is not made redundant by a future Mathlib
614	  % bump.
615	  \medskip
616	  \emph{Quasi-coherence as a corollary.} The object isomorphism just constructed
617	  exhibits \(\bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)_*\widetilde{M} \cong
618	  \widetilde{(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M)}\) as the tilde of a module. Tilde
619	  modules are quasi-coherent and quasi-coherence of \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\)-modules
620	  is closed under isomorphism, so the pushforward
621	  \(\bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)_*\widetilde{M}\) is quasi-coherent. This
622	  conclusion is stated \emph{after} the object isomorphism, of which it is a
623	  consequence — no separate ``pushforward preserves quasi-coherent'' input is
624	  needed for the affine lane.
625	\end{proof}
626	
627	\begin{remark}
628	  This single isomorphism discharges both inputs that the affine reduction below
629	  requires of the pushforward of a tilde-module.
630	  \begin{enumerate}
631	    \item \emph{The global-sections comparison.} Applying the global-sections
632	      (module-of-sections) functor to the isomorphism identifies the
633	      \(R\)-module of sections of
634	      \(\bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)_*\widetilde{M}\) with
635	      \(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\); this is exactly the \(\Gamma\)-fragment
636	      comparison used to recognise the section-level base-change map.
637	    \item \emph{Quasi-coherence of the pushforward.} The tilde of any module is
638	      quasi-coherent, and quasi-coherence of \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\)-modules is
639	      closed under isomorphism; hence
640	      \(\bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)_*\widetilde{M}\) is quasi-coherent.
641	      In particular no separate ``pushforward preserves quasi-coherent'' theorem is
642	      needed for the affine lane.
643	  \end{enumerate}
644	  Because both inputs the affine reduction asks of the pushforward are corollaries
645	  of this single object isomorphism — the section-level module identification and
646	  the quasi-coherence of the pushforward — this lemma is the sole bespoke
647	  ingredient the affine lane requires.
648	\end{remark}

651	[Affine base change]
652	  \label{lem:affine_base_change_pushforward}
653	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.affineBaseChange_pushforward_iso}
654	  \uses{def:pushforward_base_change_map}
655	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma
656	  % `lemma-affine-base-change' (\S\,Cohomology and base change, I)
657	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L906--918).
658	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $f : X \to S$ be a morphism of schemes.
659	  % Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-module.
660	  % Assume $f$ is affine.
661	  % In this case $f_*\mathcal{F} \cong Rf_*\mathcal{F}$ is
662	  % a quasi-coherent sheaf, and for every base change diagram
663	  % (\ref{equation-base-change-diagram})
664	  % we have
665	  % $$
666	  % g^*f_*\mathcal{F} = f'_*(g')^*\mathcal{F}.
667	  % $$"
668	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma ``Affine base
669	  change''.}
670	  Assume \(f : X \to S\) is an affine morphism and \(\mathcal{F}\) is a
671	  quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module. Then for every base-change square as
672	  above the canonical map of
673	  Definition~\ref{def:pushforward_base_change_map} is an isomorphism
674	  \[
675	    g^*\bigl(f_*\mathcal{F}\bigr) \;\cong\; f'_*\bigl((g')^*\mathcal{F}\bigr).
676	  \]
677	  The quasi-coherence hypothesis on \(\mathcal{F}\) is essential and is not a mere
678	  convenience: the proof works over an affine open by writing
679	  \(\mathcal{F} = \widetilde{M}\) for a module \(M\), and a general
680	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module need not be of this form
681	  \(\widetilde{M}\); without it neither the affine description of the pushforward
682	  nor the tensor-associativity computation below is available, and the conclusion
683	  fails.
684	\end{lemma}
685	
686	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "The vanishing of higher direct images is
687	% Lemma \ref{lemma-relative-affine-vanishing}.
688	% The statement is local on $S$ and $S'$. Hence we may
689	% assume $X = \Spec(A)$, $S = \Spec(R)$,
690	% $S' = \Spec(R')$ and $\mathcal{F} = \widetilde{M}$
691	% for some $A$-module $M$.

699	% $$

702	% as $R'$-modules."
703	% NOTE: Lean proof in progress. The locality reduction
704	% (lem:modules_isIso_iff_affineOpens) lands; the affine computation is reframed
705	% around full-faithfulness of the tilde functor (essential image = the
706	% quasi-coherent modules), which collapses "iso of Spec-modules" to "iso of a
707	% concrete ModuleCat R' map" and avoids all section-level SMul/Module-instance
708	% plumbing. The single Mathlib-absent ingredient is
709	% lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso (next FlatBaseChange prover target); the closing
710	% algebra is TensorProduct.AlgebraTensorModule.cancelBaseChange, present in
711	% Mathlib with no flatness. See informal/affineBaseChange_pushforward_iso.md.
712	\begin{proof}
713	  \uses{def:pushforward_base_change_map, lem:modules_isIso_iff_affineOpens, lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso}
714	  \emph{First reduction (locality).} By
715	  Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_iff_affineOpens} it suffices to prove that the
716	  base-change map of Definition~\ref{def:pushforward_base_change_map} is an
717	  isomorphism on sections over every affine open of \(S'\). Fix one such affine
718	  open. The assertion is then local on \(S\) and on \(S'\), so we may assume all
719	  schemes in sight are affine: \(X = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\),
720	  \(S = \operatorname{Spec}(R)\), \(S' = \operatorname{Spec}(R')\), with \(f\)
721	  corresponding to a ring map \(R \to A\) and \(\mathcal{F} = \widetilde{M}\) the
722	  quasi-coherent sheaf associated to an \(A\)-module \(M\). (It is here that
723	  quasi-coherence of \(\mathcal{F}\) is used: it is what licenses writing
724	  \(\mathcal{F} = \widetilde{M}\) over the affine open.) Computing the fibre product
725	  gives \(X' = \operatorname{Spec}(R' \otimes_R A)\), and the pullback
726	  \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\) is the quasi-coherent sheaf associated to
727	  the \((R' \otimes_R A)\)-module \((R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M\). In this affine
728	  situation the base-change map is a morphism \(\alpha\) of quasi-coherent
729	  \((\operatorname{Spec} R')\)-modules.
730	
731	  \medskip
732	  \emph{Reduction to a concrete module map via full-faithfulness of tilde.} The
733	  functor \(\widetilde{(-)} : \operatorname{ModuleCat}(R') \to
734	  (\operatorname{Spec} R').\mathrm{Modules}\) is fully faithful, with essential
735	  image precisely the quasi-coherent modules; equivalently, the counit comparison
736	  \(\widetilde{\Gamma(\mathcal{N})} \to \mathcal{N}\) is an isomorphism exactly when
737	  \(\mathcal{N}\) is quasi-coherent. Source and target of \(\alpha\) are
738	  quasi-coherent, so both counits are isomorphisms; by naturality of the counit, a
739	  square relates \(\alpha\) to \(\widetilde{\Gamma(\alpha)}\), and since the two
740	  vertical counit maps are isomorphisms,
741	  \[
742	    \mathrm{IsIso}\,\alpha
743	    \;\Longleftrightarrow\;
744	    \mathrm{IsIso}\bigl(\Gamma(\alpha)\bigr),
745	  \]
746	  where \(\Gamma(\alpha)\) is a concrete \(R'\)-linear map of
747	  \(\operatorname{ModuleCat}(R')\). This reframing keeps the whole computation at
748	  the level of modules and morphisms of affine schemes; in particular it requires
749	  no manipulation of section-level scalar-multiplication or module instances. The
750	  ring map underlying the pushforward at the top open is the global-sections map
751	  \(f_{\top}\) of the morphism (which for \(f = \operatorname{Spec}\varphi\) is
752	  conjugate to \(\varphi\) via the naturality of the \(\Gamma\)--\(\operatorname{Spec}\)
753	  unit), and the preimage of the top open under \(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\) is
754	  again the top open, so the identification of sections needs no transport. Any
755	  residual scalar identity in \(\operatorname{ModuleCat}(R')\) is settled by the
756	  scalar-tower compatibility of restriction of scalars.
757	
758	  \medskip
759	  \emph{Identification of the concrete map.} Under
760	  Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso}, pushforward along the affine morphism
761	  is restriction of scalars on modules and pullback along \(g\) is
762	  \(- \otimes_R R'\); accordingly \(\Gamma(\alpha)\) is identified with the
763	  canonical \(R'\)-linear comparison map between the two ways of forming the
764	  iterated tensor product. The claim therefore reduces to the associativity /
765	  cancellation isomorphism
766	  \[
767	    (R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M \;\cong\; R' \otimes_R M
768	  \]
769	  of \(R'\)-modules, which is an isomorphism (no flatness is needed in the affine
770	  case).
771	\end{proof}

782	  % references/stacks-coherent.md).
783	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Consider a cartesian diagram of schemes
784	  % $$
785	  % \xymatrix{

797	  % is an isomorphism

800	  % $$
801	  % \item if $S = \Spec(A)$ and $S' = \Spec(B)$, then

803	  % \end{enumerate}"
804	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Tag 02KH (``Flat base change''), the
805	  \(i = 0\) case.}
806	  Consider the cartesian square
807	  \[
808	    \begin{array}{ccc}
809	      X' & \xrightarrow{\;g'\;} & X \\[2pt]
810	      {\scriptstyle f'}\big\downarrow & & \big\downarrow{\scriptstyle f} \\[2pt]
811	      S' & \xrightarrow{\;g\;} & S
812	    \end{array}
813	  \]
814	  with \(\mathcal{F}\) a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module and
815	  \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\). Assume that \(g\) is flat and that \(f\)
816	  is quasi-compact and quasi-separated. Then the base-change map of
817	  Definition~\ref{def:pushforward_base_change_map} is an isomorphism
818	  \[
819	    g^*\bigl(f_*\mathcal{F}\bigr) \;\xrightarrow{\;\sim\;}\;
820	    f'_*\bigl((g')^*\mathcal{F}\bigr) = f'_*\mathcal{F}' .
821	  \]
822	  Equivalently, in the affine situation \(S = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\),
823	  \(S' = \operatorname{Spec}(B)\) with \(A \to B\) flat, the comparison map
824	  \[
825	    H^0(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B \;\xrightarrow{\;\sim\;}\;
826	    H^0(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B)
827	  \]
828	  is an isomorphism, where \(X_B = X \times_{\operatorname{Spec}(A)}
829	  \operatorname{Spec}(B)\) and \(\mathcal{F}_B\) is the pullback of
830	  \(\mathcal{F}\).
831	
832	  The hypothesis that \(\mathcal{F}\) is quasi-coherent is essential: it is what
833	  makes \(f_*\mathcal{F}\) quasi-coherent on the affine base and thus the tilde of
834	  a module, and it is what lets the affine reduction
835	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:affine_base_change_pushforward}) write \(\mathcal{F}\) as
836	  \(\widetilde{M}\) over each affine open. Without it the comparison map need not be
837	  an isomorphism.
838	\end{theorem}

896	\begin{proof}
897	  \uses{def:pushforward_base_change_map, lem:affine_base_change_pushforward}
898	  \emph{Reduction to the affine target.} The statement is local on \(S'\), so we
899	  may assume \(S\) and \(S'\) are affine, say \(S = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\) and
900	  \(S' = \operatorname{Spec}(B)\) with \(A \to B\) flat. Because \(f\) is
901	  quasi-compact and quasi-separated, \(f_*\mathcal{F}\) is quasi-coherent on
902	  \(S\); it is therefore the sheaf associated to the \(A\)-module
903	  \(H^0(S, f_*\mathcal{F}) = H^0(X, \mathcal{F})\). Likewise
904	  \(f'_*\mathcal{F}'\) is the sheaf on \(S'\) associated to the \(B\)-module
905	  \(H^0(X', \mathcal{F}')\). Since pullback along \(g\) corresponds to
906	  \(- \otimes_A B\) on modules, the base-change map becomes the comparison map of
907	  \(B\)-modules
908	  \[
909	    H^0(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B \longrightarrow H^0(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B),
910	    \qquad X_B = X \times_{\operatorname{Spec}(A)} \operatorname{Spec}(B),
911	  \]
912	  and it suffices to prove that this map is an isomorphism.
913	
914	  \medskip
915	  \emph{The separated case via {\v C}ech complexes.} Suppose first that \(X\) is
916	  separated over \(A\). Choose a finite affine open cover
917	  \(\mathcal{U} : X = U_1 \cup \dots \cup U_t\); by separatedness all finite
918	  intersections \(U_{i_0 \dots i_p}\) are again affine, so {\v C}ech cohomology
919	  computes sheaf cohomology:
920	  \(\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) = H^p(X, \mathcal{F})\). Base changing
921	  the cover yields an affine open cover
922	  \(\mathcal{U}_B : X_B = (U_1)_B \cup \dots \cup (U_t)_B\) of the again-separated
923	  \(X_B\), with the same property
924	  \(\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}_B, \mathcal{F}_B) = H^p(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B)\). The
925	  affine case (Lemma~\ref{lem:affine_base_change_pushforward}) identifies each
926	  term of the base-changed {\v C}ech complex with the original term tensored over
927	  \(A\) with \(B\); that is, there is an isomorphism of cochain complexes
928	  \[
929	    \check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}_B, \mathcal{F}_B) \;\cong\;
930	    \check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B .
931	  \]
932	  Because \(A \to B\) is flat, \(- \otimes_A B\) is an exact functor and hence
933	  commutes with the formation of cohomology of a complex; taking \(H^0\)
934	  (degree-zero cohomology) gives the desired isomorphism
935	  \(H^0(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B \cong H^0(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B)\).
936	
937	  \medskip
938	  \emph{The quasi-separated case.} In general \(X\) need only be quasi-separated.
939	  Choose a finite affine open cover \(\mathcal{U} : X = U_1 \cup \dots \cup U_t\).
940	  Now the intersections \(U_{i_0 \dots i_p}\) are quasi-compact and separated
941	  rather than affine, but they fall under the separated case already treated, so
942	  flat base change holds for each of them. One then compares the two
943	  {\v C}ech-to-cohomology spectral sequences with \(E_2\)-pages
944	  \(\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \underline{H}^q(\mathcal{F}))\) and
945	  \(\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}_B, \underline{H}^q(\mathcal{F}_B))\), converging to
946	  \(H^{p+q}(X, \mathcal{F})\) and \(H^{p+q}(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B)\) respectively.
947	  The separated case provides the term-by-term identification
948	  \(\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}_B, \underline{H}^q(\mathcal{F}_B)) =
949	  \check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \underline{H}^q(\mathcal{F})) \otimes_A B\) on the
950	  \(E_2\)-pages, and flatness of \(A \to B\) propagates this isomorphism through
951	  the spectral sequences to the abutment. In particular, in degree \(0\) one
952	  obtains \(H^0(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B \cong H^0(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B)\), as
953	  required. (For the \(i = 0\) statement at hand, one may avoid the spectral
954	  sequence entirely and argue by Mayer--Vietoris induction on the number \(t\) of
955	  cover members, reducing each step to the separated case.)
956	\end{proof}

1136	  \(R_x\)-linearity packaging of stalk maps --- consumed by the
1137	  \(\mathrm{id} \otimes g_x\) step in the proofs of

1145	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
1146	  The stalk \(M_x = \mathtt{stalk}\,M.\mathtt{presheaf}\,x\) carries its
1147	  \(R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x\)-module structure from
1148	  \texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Stalk}. The underlying additive map
1149	  \(g_x\) on stalks is the filtered colimit of the section maps \(g_U\); since
1150	  each section map is \(R(U)\)-linear and the scalar action on the stalk is the
1151	  colimit of the section actions, \(g_x\) respects the
1152	  \(R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x\)-action, which gives the linear map together with its
1153	  germ characterisation. Bijectivity is read off the underlying
1154	  \(\mathtt{Ab}\)-stalk isomorphism (a bijective linear map is a linear
1155	  equivalence), and bundling the inverse yields the linear equivalence.

1172	\begin{lemma}

1176	[Local injectivity of a whiskered localizer morphism (stalkwise, flatness-free)]
1177	  \label{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}
1178	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
1179	  Let \(R\) be a presheaf of commutative rings on a site carrying a Grothendieck
1180	  topology \(J\), and let \(J.W\) be the sheafification localizer (the locally
1181	  bijective morphisms). Concretely the site carries the
1182	  \(\mathtt{WEqualsLocallyBijective}\) property for \(\mathtt{Ab}\), so \(J.W\) is
1183	  exactly the class of \(J\)-locally bijective morphisms; this is recorded as a
1184	  typeclass hypothesis \([J.\mathtt{WEqualsLocallyBijective}\,\mathtt{Ab}]\) on
1185	  the statement. Let \(F\) be an \emph{arbitrary} presheaf of
1186	  \(R\)-modules, and let \(g : M \to N\) be a morphism of presheaves of
1187	  \(R\)-modules whose underlying morphism of presheaves of abelian groups
1188	  \((\mathtt{toPresheaf}\,R).\mathrm{map}\,g\) lies in \(J.W\). Then the
1189	  left-whiskered morphism
1190	  \[
1191	    F \triangleleft g \;\;=\;\; \mathrm{id}_F \otimes g
1192	  \]
1193	  is \emph{\(J\)-locally injective}. No flatness and no local-triviality
1194	  hypothesis on \(F\) is required.
1195	
1196	  This is the \(\mathtt{whiskerLeft}\)-field content (the injectivity half) of the
1197	  \emph{full} instance \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) (\cref{lem:jw_ismonoidal}), the
1198	  load-bearing field of the off-path \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) monoidal-category
1199	  path (arbitrary \(F\)). Its content for an arbitrary \(F\) is exactly the live lemma
1200	  \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whiskerleft_via_stalk}, which the associator's
1201	  sheafification transport consumes and which is closed \emph{unconditionally} by the
1202	  d.2 stalk--tensor commutation (\cref{lem:stalk_tensor_commutation}); the d.1/d.2
1203	  ingredients are therefore on the critical path. The two proofs recorded below --- a
1204	  sheaf-level argument specialised to locally trivial \(F\), and the general stalkwise
1205	  argument via (d.1)/(d.2) --- are retained for the historical record; the live group
1206	  law closes this obligation in its general (d.2) form through
1207	  \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whiskerleft_via_stalk}.
1208	\end{lemma}
1209	
1210	\begin{proof}
1211	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:stalk_linear_map,
1212	        lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso, lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}
1213	
1214	  \emph{PRIMARY route (\(F\) locally trivial --- the consumer's case; sheaf-level,
1215	  no stalks, no (d.2)).} Suppose \(F\) is locally trivial
1216	  (\(\mathtt{LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial}\,F\)), which is exactly the hypothesis its
1217	  sole consumer --- the associator \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso} --- supplies.
1218	  \(J\)-local injectivity of a morphism is \emph{local on a covering family}: it
1219	  suffices to produce a cover of \(X\) on each member of which \(F \triangleleft g\)
1220	  restricts to a locally injective morphism. Take a trivialising open cover
1221	  \(\{V\}\) of \(X\) for \(F\), so that \(F|_V \cong \mathcal{O}_V\) on each \(V\).
1222	  On such a \(V\):
1223	  \begin{itemize}
1224	    \item the substrate--restriction compatibility \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}
1225	      gives a canonical isomorphism
1226	      \[
1227	        (F \triangleleft g)\big|_V \;\cong\; (F|_V) \triangleleft (g|_V)
1228	      \]
1229	      --- restriction along the open immersion \(V \hookrightarrow X\) commutes with
1230	      \(\otimes\), and the whiskering \(F \triangleleft g = \mathrm{id}_F \otimes g\)
1231	      is \(\otimes\) with an identity, so it restricts to \(\mathrm{id}_{F|_V}
1232	      \otimes (g|_V) = (F|_V) \triangleleft (g|_V)\);
1233	    \item the trivialisation \(F|_V \cong \mathcal{O}_V\) together with the left
1234	      unitor \cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso} (\(\mathcal{O}_V \otimes_V (-) \cong
1235	      (-)\)) identifies \((F|_V) \triangleleft (g|_V) \cong g|_V\).
1236	  \end{itemize}
1237	  Composing, \((F \triangleleft g)|_V\) is isomorphic to \(g|_V\). Now \(g \in J.W\),
1238	  and membership in \(J.W\) (local bijectivity) is stable under restriction along an
1239	  open immersion, so \(g|_V \in J.W\); in particular \(g|_V\) is \(J\)-locally
1240	  injective. An isomorphism preserves local injectivity, hence \((F \triangleleft
1241	  g)|_V\) is locally injective on \(V\). As the \(\{V\}\) cover \(X\) and local
1242	  injectivity is local on the cover, \(F \triangleleft g\) is \(J\)-locally
1243	  injective. \emph{No stalks and no (d.2) stalk--tensor commutation enter}; the
1244	  single comparison lemma \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} (a presheaf-level
1245	  comparison, decomposed into its two open-immersion base-change halves) carries the
1246	  argument, and thereby moves \emph{onto} the critical path.
1247	
1248	  This is \emph{not} the section-level route that tensors a bare section map \(g(V)\)
1249	  --- only injective --- and stalls on a \(\mathrm{Tor}_1\)/flatness obligation. Here
1250	  the argument is sheaf-level and uses that \(g\) is locally \emph{bijective} (a
1251	  \(J.W\) morphism), reduced through the unitor to \(g|_V\) (again a \(J.W\)
1252	  morphism); a mere injection is never tensored, so no flatness is needed.
1253	
1254	  \emph{FALLBACK route (arbitrary \(F\); stalkwise, required only for the
1255	  full-generality route~(e) \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) path).} For an
1256	  \emph{arbitrary} (not necessarily locally trivial) \(F\), local injectivity is
1257	  proved stalkwise, by the argument Mathlib uses for fixed-base presheaves in
1258	  \(\mathtt{CategoryTheory.Sites.Point.IsMonoidalW}\)
1259	  (\cref{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}), ported to \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\). A
1260	  morphism in \(J.W\) is, on the topological site of \(X\), a \emph{stalkwise
1261	  isomorphism}: at each point \(x\) the induced map on stalks \(g_x\) is an
1262	  isomorphism. The presheaf-of-modules tensor is sectionwise, so it commutes with
1263	  the stalk (a filtered colimit), giving
1264	  \[
1265	    (F \triangleleft g)_x
1266	      \;=\; \mathrm{id}_{F_x} \otimes_{R_x} g_x
1267	      \;\colon\; F_x \otimes_{R_x} M_x \;\longrightarrow\; F_x \otimes_{R_x} N_x .
1268	  \]
1269	  Tensoring an isomorphism \(g_x\) by the identity \(\mathrm{id}_{F_x}\) yields an
1270	  isomorphism --- no flatness is needed, since \(g_x\) is invertible, not merely
1271	  injective. Hence \(F \triangleleft g\) is a stalkwise isomorphism, in particular
1272	  locally bijective, in particular locally injective, for \emph{any} \(F\).
1273	
1274	  The stalkwise computation rests on three ingredients with distinct status at the
1275	  pinned commit:
1276	  \begin{enumerate}
1277	    \item[(d.1-done)] the \(R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x\)-linear stalk-map packaging
1278	      (\(\mathtt{stalkLinearMap}\) and its companions, \cref{lem:stalk_linear_map}):
1279	      a morphism of presheaves of modules induces an \(R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x\)-linear
1280	      map on stalks, characterised on germs, that becomes a linear equivalence once
1281	      the underlying \(\mathtt{Ab}\)-stalk map is an isomorphism. This is built
1282	      project-side, axiom-clean, and furnishes the \(\mathrm{id} \otimes g_x\) step
1283	      above.
1284	    \item[(d.1-bridge)] (remaining) the site-\(J.W\) \(\Longleftrightarrow\)
1285	      stalkwise-iso characterisation on \(\mathtt{Opens}\,X\): that a morphism lies
1286	      in \(J.W\) iff its stalk maps are isomorphisms at every point. The concrete
1287	      Mathlib assembly route uses the \(\mathtt{WEqualsLocallyBijective}\) structure
1288	      together with the topological stalk criteria --- local injectivity
1289	      \(\Longleftrightarrow\) injectivity on stalks and local surjectivity
1290	      \(\Longleftrightarrow\) surjectivity on stalks --- bridging the site-level
1291	      \(\mathtt{Presheaf.IsLocallyInjective}\) / \(\mathtt{IsLocallySurjective}\)
1292	      to the \(\mathtt{TopCat.Presheaf}\) stalk versions. This half is still
1293	      Mathlib-absent at the \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\) level.
1294	    \item[(d.2)] (remaining) the commutation \((A \otimes^{p} B)_x \cong A_x
1295	      \otimes_{R_x} B_x\) of the stalk (a filtered colimit) with the relative
1296	      module-presheaf tensor, under which \((F \triangleleft g)_x\) is identified
1297	      with \(\mathrm{lTensor}\,F_x\,(g_x) = \mathrm{id}_{F_x} \otimes g_x\) (using
1298	      that \(\mathtt{tensorLeft}\) / \(\mathtt{tensorRight}\) preserve filtered
1299	      colimits over a module category). This is genuinely Mathlib-absent over a
1300	      \emph{varying} ring and is the largest remaining piece.
1301	  \end{enumerate}
1302	  Mathlib supplies the analogues for presheaves valued in a fixed monoidal concrete
1303	  category, but not for modules over a varying ring; porting (d.1-bridge) and (d.2)
1304	  to \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\) is the remaining work. Once (d.2) lands, the
1305	  conclusion is immediate and flatness-free: by
1306	  \(\mathtt{stalkLinearMap\_bijective\_of\_isIso}\) the stalk map \(g_x\) is a
1307	  linear equivalence, and the linear-equivalence \(\mathrm{lTensor}\) by \(F_x\)
1308	  carries it to an isomorphism, finishing the proof.
1309	
1310	  This stalkwise fallback is required \emph{only} if the full-generality
1311	  \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) / \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) route~(e) --- the
1312	  monoidal category on \emph{all} of \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), for an
1313	  arbitrary whisker object \(F\) --- is later wanted. For the relative Picard group
1314	  law the whisker objects are locally trivial, and the PRIMARY route above suffices,
1315	  trading the Mathlib-absent (d.2) for the single comparison
1316	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}.
1317	\end{proof}

1327	\begin{lemma}
1328	% SUPERSEDED route (route-(d)/(e) whiskering/stalk machinery); pending deletion once the assoc re-route (morphism-level descent) lands. Declaration still present and still backing the current `tensorObj_assoc_iso` proof.
1329	[Flatness-free whiskering preserves the sheafification localizer]
1330	  \label{lem:whisker_of_W}
1331	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}
1332	  With notation as in \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}: for an
1333	  \emph{arbitrary} presheaf of \(R\)-modules \(F\) and a morphism \(g\) whose
1334	  underlying additive-presheaf map lies in \(J.W\), both whiskerings again lie in
1335	  \(J.W\):
1336	  \[
1337	    g \in J.W \;\Longrightarrow\; F \triangleleft g \in J.W,
1338	    \qquad
1339	    g \in J.W \;\Longrightarrow\; g \triangleright F \in J.W .
1340	  \]
1341	  These two statements are precisely the \(\mathtt{whiskerLeft}\) and
1342	  \(\mathtt{whiskerRight}\) data required by the class
1343	  \(\mathtt{MorphismProperty.IsMonoidal}\) (\cref{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}) for
1344	  the morphism property \(J.W\); together they furnish the instance
1345	  \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) of \cref{lem:jw_ismonoidal}. No flatness and no
1346	  local triviality is used --- they are the flatness-free replacements for the
1347	  flat variants of \cref{lem:flat_whisker_localizer}.
1348	\end{lemma}
1349	
1350	\begin{proof}
1351	  \uses{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}
1352	  By the locally-bijective characterisation of \(J.W\), it suffices to show
1353	  \(F \triangleleft g\) is both locally injective and locally surjective.
1354	  \emph{Local surjectivity} is free and needs no hypothesis on \(F\): the presheaf
1355	  tensor is right exact, so \(F \otimes^{p} -\) carries the locally-zero cokernel
1356	  of \(g\) to the cokernel of \(F \triangleleft g\), which is therefore locally
1357	  zero (Mathlib \(\mathtt{isLocallySurjective\_whiskerLeft}\)). \emph{Local
1358	  injectivity} is \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}. The two together
1359	  give \(F \triangleleft g \in J.W\). The right-whiskered statement
1360	  \(g \triangleright F\) follows by conjugating \(F \triangleleft g\) with the
1361	  braiding of the symmetric presheaf monoidal structure, which is an isomorphism
1362	  and hence preserves membership in \(J.W\).
1363	\end{proof}

1377	[The \(J.W\)-monoidality instance and the localized monoidal substrate (route~(e) target)]
1378	  \label{lem:jw_ismonoidal}
1379	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:whisker_of_W}
1380	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. The sheafification localizer \(J.W\) on
1381	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,\mathcal{O}_X\) is \emph{monoidal}: it carries an
1382	  instance
1383	  \[
1384	    J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal},
1385	  \]
1386	  whose two fields are the whiskering-stability lemmas \cref{lem:whisker_of_W}
1387	  (and which is multiplicative because sheafification inverts identities and
1388	  composites). Consequently, by Mathlib's
1389	  \(\mathtt{Localization.Monoidal.LocalizedMonoidal}\)
1390	  (\cref{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}) applied to the already-monoidal category
1391	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,\mathcal{O}_X\), the localization functor
1392	  \(L = \mathtt{PresheafOfModules.sheafification}\) (which is the localization at
1393	  \(J.W\)), and the unit isomorphism \(L(\mathbf{1}) \cong \mathcal{O}_X\), the
1394	  category
1395	  \[
1396	    \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
1397	      \;=\; \mathtt{SheafOfModules}\,X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}
1398	  \]
1399	  acquires a full \(\MonoidalCategory\) structure whose tensor is the substrate
1400	  \(\otimes_X\) (\cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}) and whose associator,
1401	  left/right unitors, braiding and \emph{all} coherence (pentagon, triangle,
1402	  naturalities) are derived by the API. This is the route~(e) target: the
1403	  associator \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}, the unitors
1404	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso} and the braiding \cref{lem:tensorobj_comm_iso} are
1405	  then components of this derived monoidal structure rather than hand-assembled
1406	  composites.
1407	
1408	  This block states the route~(e) construction; it depends on the sole open obligation
1409	  \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}. It mirrors Mathlib's
1410	  \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\) construction one categorical level up.
1411	\end{lemma}

1413	\begin{proof}
1414	  \uses{lem:whisker_of_W}
1415	  Multiplicativity of \(J.W\) (closure under identities and composition) is part
1416	  of its being a localizer (\(\mathtt{WEqualsLocallyBijective}\)). The two
1417	  whiskering-stability fields of \(\mathtt{MorphismProperty.IsMonoidal}\) are
1418	  exactly the left- and right-whiskering statements of \cref{lem:whisker_of_W}.

1420	  modules is a localization at \(J.W\) (the underlying identification of
1421	  sheafification with localization at the locally-bijective class), and the unit
1422	  presheaf sheafifies to \(\mathcal{O}_X\); feeding these to

1424	  \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) with all coherence derived, as in
1425	  \cref{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}. No \(\mathtt{MonoidalClosed}\) structure enters
1426	  at any point.

1433	[Associator for $\otimes_X$ (unconditional)]
1434	  \label{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}

1436	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj,

1438	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let
1439	  \(M, N, P \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). There is an isomorphism
1440	  \[
1441	    (M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P
1442	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;

1444	  \]

1459	\begin{proof}
1460	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj,
1461	        lem:islocallyinjective_whiskerleft_via_stalk}
1462	  \textbf{Realization (sheafification transport, unconditional via d.2).} The
1463	  associator is the sheafification transport of the presheaf-of-modules associator
1464	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategoryStruct.associator}\), which exists for
1465	  \emph{all} presheaves of modules over the varying ring. The transport requires the
1466	  single left-whisker \(F \triangleleft \mathtt{toSheafify}\) to lie in the
1467	  sheafification localizer \(J.W\) for the relevant \(F\); that single open
1468	  left-whiskering obligation \(\mathtt{isLocallyInjective\_whiskerLeft\_of\_W}\) is
1469	  closed \emph{unconditionally} --- for an arbitrary whiskering object, with no
1470	  flatness and no local triviality --- by the d.2 stalk--tensor commutation
1471	  \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whiskerleft_via_stalk} (\cref{sec:tensorobj_stalk_tensor}):
1472	  a \(J.W\)-morphism \(g\) is a stalkwise isomorphism, and by the d.2 commutation
1473	  \(\bigl(F \triangleleft g\bigr)_x \cong \mathrm{id}_{F_x} \otimes g_x\) is again an
1474	  isomorphism, so \(F \triangleleft g \in J.W\). Hence the associator is itself
1475	  unconditional and axiom-clean once d.2 (\cref{lem:stalk_tensor_commutation}) is
1476	  formalized; it does \emph{not} require the full \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) instance
1477	  or the \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) monoidal category --- only the one left-whisker
1478	  lemma --- and no sectionwise flatness enters. The local-gluing description below
1479	  records the equivalent concrete local realization through the
1480	  restriction-compatibility comparison \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}.
1481	
1482	  Concretely, the same isomorphism is described by gluing canonical local isomorphisms
1483	  through the restriction-compatibility comparison \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}.
1484	  Fix a
1485	  common open cover \(\{U\}\) of \(X\). On each member \(U\) form the canonical
1486	  composite
1487	  \[
1488	    \bigl((M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P\bigr)\big|_U
1489	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
1490	    \bigl(M|_U \otimes_U N|_U\bigr) \otimes_U P|_U
1491	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
1492	    M|_U \otimes_U \bigl(N|_U \otimes_U P|_U\bigr)
1493	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
1494	    \bigl(M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P)\bigr)\big|_U ,
1495	  \]
1496	  in which:
1497	  \begin{itemize}
1498	    \item the first arrow is two applications of the restriction-compatibility
1499	      isomorphism \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} along the open immersion
1500	      \(U \hookrightarrow X\): first \((M \otimes_X N)|_U \cong M|_U \otimes_U
1501	      N|_U\) tensored with \(P|_U\), then \(\bigl((M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X
1502	      P\bigr)|_U \cong (M \otimes_X N)|_U \otimes_U P|_U\);
1503	    \item the middle arrow is the \emph{canonical presheaf-level associator}
1504	      \(\alpha\) of the (symmetric) monoidal category
1505	      \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,\mathcal{O}_U\)
1506	      (\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategoryStruct.associator}\),
1507	      \cref{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}(i)), read on the restricted modules;
1508	    \item the third arrow is again two applications of
1509	      \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}, in the opposite direction.
1510	  \end{itemize}
1511	  Each of the three arrows is a \emph{canonical} isomorphism: the
1512	  restriction-compatibility comparison \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} is induced
1513	  by the open immersion with no choices, hence natural in its module arguments, and
1514	  the presheaf associator \(\alpha\) is a fixed natural transformation. Therefore,
1515	  on a double overlap \(U \cap U'\), restricting the \(U\)-composite and the
1516	  \(U'\)-composite to \(U \cap U'\) yields the same morphism: each of the three
1517	  arrows commutes with the further restriction to \(U \cap U'\) by the naturality of
1518	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} and of the presheaf associator, so the two local
1519	  composites agree on the overlap.
1520	
1521	  Because the internal hom \(\mathcal{H}om(-,-)\) of sheaves of
1522	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules is itself a sheaf on \(X\), a family of morphisms defined
1523	  on the members of an open cover and agreeing on the overlaps glues to a single
1524	  global morphism. Applying this both to the local composites above and to their
1525	  local inverses produces a global morphism
1526	  \((M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P \to M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P)\) whose restriction
1527	  to each \(U\) is the local composite, together with a two-sided inverse glued from
1528	  the local inverses; hence the glued morphism is a global isomorphism --- the
1529	  associator. The single non-formal input of this re-route is the
1530	  restriction-compatibility comparison \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}; the presheaf
1531	  associator (\cref{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}(i)) and the sheaf-of-modules
1532	  internal-hom gluing are already available.
1533	
1534	  Note that, unlike the pointwise-existence statement
1535	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial} --- a \emph{proposition} proved
1536	  cover-pointwise with \emph{no} overlap obligation --- the associator is
1537	  \emph{global data}, a single isomorphism of sheaves on all of \(X\). Assembling it
1538	  from local pieces is therefore genuinely stronger than that pattern: the
1539	  overlap-naturality compatibility of the local composites must be discharged before
1540	  gluing, and is discharged above. The locally-trivial hypotheses of the statement
1541	  are not consumed; the comparison \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} is applied to the
1542	  global, untrivialised restrictions \((M \otimes_X N)|_U\), so no free-cover
1543	  specialisation can replace it. What this proof does \emph{not} require is the
1544	  \emph{full} \((J.W).\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) instance or the
1545	  \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) monoidal category
1546	  (\cref{lem:whisker_of_W,lem:jw_ismonoidal}): only the single left-whisker obligation
1547	  \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whiskerleft_via_stalk}, closed via the d.2 stalk--tensor
1548	  commutation \cref{lem:stalk_tensor_commutation}, is consumed by the sheafification
1549	  transport. The group law on \(\otimes\)-iso-classes
1550	  consumes only the \emph{existence} of the resulting isomorphism, never its pentagon,
1551	  triangle, or naturality coherence.
1552	\end{proof}
1553

1611	[Inverse of an invertible module]
1612	  \label{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible}
1613	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.exists_tensorObj_inverse}
1614	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial,
1615	        lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}
1616	  % NOTE: ON the critical path under the PRIMARY locally-trivial group law (it
1617	  % supplies inverses to lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup, in whose uses it sits).
1618	  % Only under the route-(e) fallback is it dispensable: there the group law uses
1619	  % the tensor-invertibility predicate IsInvertible (def:scheme_modules_isinvertible),

1623	  % modules), Lemma~lemma-constructions-invertible (item~2: the dual of an

1625	  % Definition~definition-pic (the Picard group as the abelian group of
