1628	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1629	  % "\begin{lemma}
1630	  % \label{lemma-constructions-invertible}
1631	  % Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space.
1632	  % ...
1633	  % \item If $\mathcal{L}$ is an invertible $\mathcal{O}_X$-module, then so is
1634	  % $\SheafHom_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{O}_X)$ and the evaluation map
1635	  % $\mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X}
1636	  % \SheafHom_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{O}_X) \to \mathcal{O}_X$
1637	  % is an isomorphism.
1638	  % ...
1639	  % \begin{definition}
1640	  % \label{definition-pic}
1641	  % Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space.
1642	  % The {\it Picard group} $\Pic(X)$ of $X$ is the
1643	  % abelian group whose elements are isomorphism classes of
1644	  % invertible $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules, with addition
1645	  % corresponding to tensor product.
1646	  % \end{definition}"
1647	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag~01CR, Lemma~\texttt{lemma-constructions-%
1648	  invertible} (item~2, the dual of an invertible is invertible and the contraction
1649	  is an isomorphism) and Definition~\texttt{definition-pic} (the Picard group is
1650	  the abelian group of iso-classes of invertible \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules under
1651	  tensor product).}
1652	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let \(L \in \texttt{LineBundle}\,X\) be a line
1653	  bundle. The dual
1654	  \[
1655	    L^{-1} \;\;:=\;\; \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_X}(L, \mathcal{O}_X)
1656	  \]
1657	  is again a line bundle on \(X\), and there is a canonical natural
1658	  isomorphism
1659	  \(\varepsilon_L : L \otimes_X L^{-1} \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{O}_X\).
1660	  Consequently every line bundle has a two-sided tensor inverse under
1661	  \(\otimes_X\), and the monoid \(\bigl(\texttt{LineBundle}\,X / \sim, \otimes_X,
1662	  \mathcal{O}_X\bigr)\) of isomorphism classes is an abelian group ---
1663	  the Picard group \(\Pic(X)\) (Stacks tag 01CR).
1664	\end{lemma}
1665	
1666	\begin{proof}
1667	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial, lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso,
1668	        lem:tensorobj_unit_iso, lem:internal_hom_eval, lem:dual_isLocallyTrivial}
1669	  \textbf{Infrastructure-blocked.} This statement is \emph{not} realizable with the
1670	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module infrastructure presently available: its construction
1671	  depends on a \emph{sheaf internal hom of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules}
1672	  \(\mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_X}(L, \mathcal{O}_X)\), an object that does not yet
1673	  exist at the presheaf, sheaf, or general-categorical level (there is no
1674	  monoidal-closed / internal-hom / dual structure on
1675	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\) or \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules}\), and no object-level
1676	  descent assembling a global \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module from local pieces). The dual
1677	  object \(L^{-1}\) therefore cannot even be \emph{named} until that infrastructure
1678	  is built. The construction of the required internal hom, its dual, and its
1679	  evaluation is decomposed into individually-formalizable sub-steps in
1680	  \cref{sec:tensorobj_dual_infra} (\cref{def:presheaf_internal_hom} through
1681	  \cref{lem:dual_isLocallyTrivial}); the present lemma is realized once those land,
1682	  and its Lean body is a placeholder until then.
1683	
1684	  We record the intended mathematical route, all three steps of which are correct
1685	  modulo the missing object. \emph{The route is as follows.} Take
1686	  \(L^{-1} := \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_X}(L, \mathcal{O}_X)\) to be the
1687	  sheaf-level dual of \cref{lem:internal_hom_isSheaf}, and let
1688	  \(\varepsilon_L : L \otimes_X L^{-1} \to \mathcal{O}_X\) be the evaluation of
1689	  \cref{lem:internal_hom_eval}.
1690	
1691	  \emph{Step 1 (\(L^{-1}\) is a line bundle).} Local triviality of \(L\)
1692	  (\(\mathtt{LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial}\,L\)) furnishes an open cover \(\{U\}\) of
1693	  \(X\) with trivialisations \(L|_U \cong \mathcal{O}_U\). The internal hom commutes
1694	  with restriction along the open immersion \(U \hookrightarrow X\), so on each \(U\)
1695	  \[
1696	    L^{-1}\big|_U \;\cong\; \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_U}(L|_U, \mathcal{O}_U)
1697	                 \;\cong\; \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_U}(\mathcal{O}_U, \mathcal{O}_U)
1698	                 \;\cong\; \mathcal{O}_U ,
1699	  \]
1700	  the last identification being the evaluation-at-\(1\) isomorphism for the dual of a
1701	  free rank-one module. Hence \(L^{-1}\) is locally trivial of rank one, i.e.\ an
1702	  object of \(\texttt{LineBundle}\,X\).
1703	
1704	  \emph{Step 2 (the contraction is a local isomorphism).} The evaluation/contraction
1705	  morphism
1706	  \[
1707	    \varepsilon_L \;\colon\; L \otimes_X L^{-1}
1708	      \;=\; L \otimes_X \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_X}(L, \mathcal{O}_X)
1709	      \;\longrightarrow\; \mathcal{O}_X,
1710	    \qquad s \otimes \phi \longmapsto \phi(s),
1711	  \]
1712	  is a morphism in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). Restricting to a trivialising
1713	  open \(U\), the restriction-compatibility isomorphism \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}
1714	  commutes \(\otimes_X\) past \((-)|_U\):
1715	  \[
1716	    (L \otimes_X L^{-1})\big|_U \;\cong\; L|_U \otimes_U L^{-1}|_U
1717	      \;\cong\; \mathcal{O}_U \otimes_U \mathcal{O}_U ,
1718	  \]
1719	  using the trivialisations of Step~1. Under these identifications \(\varepsilon_L|_U\)
1720	  becomes the contraction \(\mathcal{O}_U \otimes_U \mathcal{O}_U \to \mathcal{O}_U\) of
1721	  the free rank-one module with its dual, which is precisely the left unitor
1722	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso} (\(\mathcal{O}_U \otimes_U (-) \cong (-)\)) and hence an
1723	  isomorphism. Thus \(\varepsilon_L|_U\) is an isomorphism on each member of the cover.
1724	
1725	  \emph{Step 3 (glue to a global isomorphism).} The local trivialisations are induced
1726	  by the open immersions with no choices, so the local contraction isomorphisms
1727	  \(\varepsilon_L|_U\) agree on the overlaps \(U \cap U'\) by the naturality of
1728	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} and of the unitor under further restriction. As
1729	  ``being an isomorphism'' is local on \(X\) --- a morphism of sheaves of
1730	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules whose restriction to every member of an open cover is an
1731	  isomorphism is itself an isomorphism --- the globally-defined \(\varepsilon_L\) is an
1732	  isomorphism \(L \otimes_X L^{-1} \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{O}_X\). Equivalently, the
1733	  local inverses glue (they agree on overlaps for the same reason) to a two-sided
1734	  inverse of \(\varepsilon_L\).
1735	
1736	  Consequently, \emph{once \(L^{-1}\) and \(\varepsilon_L\) are available from
1737	  \cref{sec:tensorobj_dual_infra}}, every line bundle \(L\) has the two-sided tensor
1738	  inverse \(L^{-1}\) under \(\otimes_X\), and the monoid of \(\otimes\)-iso-classes of
1739	  line bundles is an abelian group --- the Picard group \(\Pic(X)\) (Stacks tag 01CR).
1740	  The local-triviality of \(L^{-1}\) (Step~1) is \cref{lem:dual_isLocallyTrivial} and
1741	  the local-iso\(\Rightarrow\)global-iso passage (Step~3) is the already-closed
1742	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}; the single non-available input is the dual object
1743	  and its evaluation of \cref{sec:tensorobj_dual_infra}.
1744	\end{proof}

1790	\begin{lemma}
1791	[\(\pi_T^*\) is a tensor-functor]
1792	  \label{lem:pullback_compatible_with_tensorobj}
1793	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.LineBundle.OnProduct.pullback_tensorObj_iso}
1794	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}
1795	  % NOTE: pullback preserves the structure sheaf and tensor products (and hence
1796	  % tensor-invertibility) by the standard pullback-tensor compatibility of
1797	  % \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules (Stacks \texttt{lemma-tensor-product-pullback} /
1798	  % \texttt{lemma-pullback-invertible}), independently of the open-immersion
1799	  % restriction-compatibility iso.
1800	  With notation as in \cref{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}, the pullback
1801	  functor
1802	  \[
1803	    \pi_T^{*} \;\;\colon\;\; \texttt{LineBundle}\,T \;\longrightarrow\;
1804	                          \texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T
1805	  \]
1806	  of \cref{def:pullback_along_projection} is a (strong) monoidal
1807	  functor: there is a canonical natural isomorphism
1808	  \(\pi_T^*(\mathcal{N} \otimes_T \mathcal{N'})
1809	     \xrightarrow{\sim}
1810	   \pi_T^* \mathcal{N} \otimes_{C \times_S T} \pi_T^* \mathcal{N'}\)
1811	  for \(\mathcal{N}, \mathcal{N'} \in \texttt{LineBundle}\,T\), and a
1812	  canonical isomorphism
1813	  \(\pi_T^* \mathcal{O}_T \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{O}_{C \times_S T}\),

1815	  \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\) functors via the
1816	  pullback-tensor-compatibility of \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules.

1818	
1819	\begin{proof}
1820	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}

1822	  \(\mathcal{N'}\) trivialise simultaneously, the assertion reduces to
1823	  the standard fact that the extension of scalars
1824	  \(B \otimes_A (M \otimes_A M')
1825	   \;\cong\; (B \otimes_A M) \otimes_B (B \otimes_A M')\)
1826	  for a ring map \(A \to B\) and \(A\)-modules \(M, M'\). This affine
1827	  identity glues across an affine trivialising cover of \(T\): on each
1828	  affine open where \(\mathcal{N}\) and \(\mathcal{N}'\) are free of rank one
1829	  over \(A\), both sides evaluate to the free rank-one \(B\)-module, so
1830	  the comparison is an isomorphism. No flatness of \(A \to B\) is required
1831	  at this level, as the modules involved are locally free. The compatibility
1832	  with the unit, the associator, and the unitors is verified pointwise on the
1833	  same cover.

1836	\section{The varying-ring stalk--tensor commutation (d.2) and the unconditional
1837	left-whiskering}
1838	\label{sec:tensorobj_stalk_tensor}
1839	
1840	This section supplies the one genuinely Mathlib-absent ingredient on which the
1841	\emph{unconditional} associator \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso} rests: the commutation
1842	of the stalk functor with the relative-ring tensor product of presheaves of modules
1843	(``d.2''). Together with the already-built \(R_x\)-linear stalk comparison
1844	(\cref{lem:stalk_linear_map}, ``d.1'') it closes the single open left-whiskering
1845	obligation for an \emph{arbitrary} whiskering object --- with no flatness and no
1846	local-triviality hypothesis --- which is exactly what makes the sheafification
1847	transport of the presheaf associator work for all modules. The construction is to be
1848	formalized in the dedicated Lean file
1849	\(\mathtt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/TensorObjSubstrate/StalkTensor.lean}\).
1850	
1851	\begin{lemma}
1852	
1853	\leanok
1854	[Stalk of a tensor product (d.2): varying-ring stalk--tensor commutation]
1855	  \label{lem:stalk_tensor_commutation}
1856	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.stalkTensorIso}
1857	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:stalk_linear_map}
1858	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Tag (Modules on Ringed Spaces, \S Tensor product),
1859	  % Lemma lemma-stalk-tensor-product (stalk of a tensor product of modules is the
1860	  % tensor product of the stalks over the stalk ring)
1861	  % (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L2332-L2344).
1862	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1863	  % "\begin{lemma}
1864	  % \label{lemma-stalk-tensor-product}
1865	  % Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space.
1866	  % Let $\mathcal{F}$, $\mathcal{G}$ be $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules.
1867	  % Let $x \in X$. There is a canonical isomorphism
1868	  % of $\mathcal{O}_{X, x}$-modules
1869	  % $$
1870	  % (\mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G})_x
1871	  % =
1872	  % \mathcal{F}_x \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_{X, x}} \mathcal{G}_x
1873	  % $$
1874	  % functorial in $\mathcal{F}$ and $\mathcal{G}$.
1875	  % \end{lemma}"
1876	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Modules on Ringed Spaces,
1877	  Lemma~\texttt{lemma-stalk-tensor-product}.}
1878	  Let \(R : (\mathtt{Opens}\,X)^{op} \to \mathtt{CommRingCat}\) be the structure
1879	  presheaf of a scheme \(X\), and let \(A, B\) be presheaves of
1880	  \(R\)-modules (objects of \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,(R \circ
1881	  \mathtt{forget_2}\,\mathtt{CommRingCat}\,\mathtt{RingCat})\)). For each point
1882	  \(x \in X\) there is a canonical isomorphism of \((R \circ
1883	  \mathtt{forget_2}).\mathtt{stalk}\,x\)-modules
1884	  \[
1885	    (A \otimes^{p} B).\mathtt{stalk}\,x
1886	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
1887	    A.\mathtt{stalk}\,x \;\otimes_{R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x}\; B.\mathtt{stalk}\,x,
1888	  \]
1889	  natural in \(A\) and \(B\), where \(\otimes^{p}\) is the presheaf-of-modules tensor
1890	  product \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\).

1893	\begin{proof}
1894	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:stalk_linear_map}
1895	  \leanok
1896	  The stalk at \(x\) is the filtered colimit over the neighbourhoods \(U \ni x\) of
1897	  the sections functor, and sectionwise the tensor product is computed pointwise:
1898	  \((A \otimes^{p} B)(U) = A(U) \otimes_{R(U)} B(U)\)
1899	  (\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj\_obj}\)). The neighbourhood system
1900	  \(\{U \ni x\}\) is cofiltered under inclusion, so the colimit is filtered. The
1901	  isomorphism is assembled in five named stages. Stages (i)--(ii) construct the
1902	  forward additive comparison map and its germ characterisation; stages (iii)--(v)
1903	  upgrade it to an \(R_x\)-linear map, construct the reverse map, and check mutual
1904	  inversion.
1905	
1906	  \emph{(i) Per-neighbourhood balanced bilinear map and its section-level descent}
1907	  (\(\mathtt{stalkTensorBilin}\), \(\mathtt{stalkTensorDescU}\)). For a fixed
1908	  neighbourhood \(U \ni x\) the assignment
1909	  \[
1910	    (a, b) \;\longmapsto\; \mathrm{germ}_x\,a \,\otimes\, \mathrm{germ}_x\,b,
1911	    \qquad a \in A(U),\ b \in B(U),
1912	  \]
1913	  is \(R(U)\)-balanced bilinear into \(A_x \otimes_{R_x} B_x\): it is additive in each
1914	  variable, and for \(r \in R(U)\) the two ways of moving \(r\) across the tensor agree
1915	  after passing to germs, since \(\mathrm{germ}_x(r \cdot a) = (\mathrm{germ}_x r)
1916	  \cdot \mathrm{germ}_x a\) and the stalk tensor is balanced over
1917	  \(R_x = R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x\) (the germ--scalar compatibility of
1918	  \cref{lem:stalk_linear_map}). By the universal property of the relative tensor
1919	  product it descends to an additive map on the section module
1920	  \[
1921	    \mathtt{stalkTensorDescU}_U \;\colon\;
1922	      (A \otimes^{p} B)(U) = A(U) \otimes_{R(U)} B(U)
1923	        \;\longrightarrow\; A_x \otimes_{R_x} B_x,
1924	    \qquad a \otimes b \longmapsto \mathrm{germ}_x a \otimes \mathrm{germ}_x b.
1925	  \]
1926	
1927	  \emph{(ii) Colimit descent to the forward comparison map}
1928	  (\(\mathtt{stalkTensorDesc}\), \(\mathtt{stalkTensorDesc\_germ\_tmul}\)). The
1929	  maps \(\mathtt{stalkTensorDescU}_U\) are compatible with the restriction maps of the
1930	  presheaf \(A \otimes^{p} B\) as \(U\) shrinks toward \(x\) (a smaller neighbourhood
1931	  computes the same germs), so they form a cocone over the filtered diagram
1932	  \(\{(A \otimes^{p} B)(U)\}_{U \ni x}\). The induced map out of the colimit is the
1933	  forward additive comparison map
1934	  \[
1935	    \mathtt{stalkTensorDesc} \;\colon\;
1936	      (A \otimes^{p} B).\mathtt{stalk}\,x \;\longrightarrow\; A_x \otimes_{R_x} B_x,
1937	  \]
1938	  characterised on germs of simple tensors by
1939	  \(\mathtt{stalkTensorDesc}\bigl(\mathrm{germ}_x(a \otimes b)\bigr) =
1940	  \mathrm{germ}_x a \otimes \mathrm{germ}_x b\)
1941	  (\(\mathtt{stalkTensorDesc\_germ\_tmul}\)). This is the forward half of the asserted
1942	  isomorphism (\cref{lem:stalk_tensor_desc_forward}).
1943	
1944	  \emph{(iii) \(R_x\)-linearity packaging} (\(\mathtt{stalkTensorLinearMap}\)). The
1945	  additive map of (ii) is upgraded to an \(R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x\)-linear map: on a germ
1946	  \(\mathrm{germ}_x r\) of a section \(r \in R(U)\) one checks
1947	  \(\mathtt{stalkTensorDesc}\bigl((\mathrm{germ}_x r)\cdot \xi\bigr) =
1948	  (\mathrm{germ}_x r)\cdot \mathtt{stalkTensorDesc}(\xi)\) on germs of simple tensors
1949	  and extends by additivity. The one subtlety is a \emph{carrier-duality obstacle}:
1950	  the section-level tensor \(A(U) \otimes_{R(U)} B(U)\) is a module over the
1951	  \(\mathtt{RingCat}\) carrier \((R \circ \mathtt{forget_2})(U)\), whereas the natural
1952	  scalar \(r : R(U)\) lives in the \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\) carrier \(R(U)\); the two
1953	  carriers are canonically identified but not definitionally equal, so the
1954	  scalar-pulling lemma \(\mathtt{TensorProduct.smul\_tmul'}\) applies only after
1955	  inserting a canonical \(\mathtt{RingEquiv}\) bridge between the
1956	  \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\) and \(\mathtt{RingCat}\) carriers. Once that bridge is
1957	  in place the linearity computation mirrors the germ-representative pattern used
1958	  for the d.1 stalk map \(\mathtt{stalkLinearMap}\) (\cref{lem:stalk_linear_map}).
1959	
1960	  \emph{(iv) The reverse map.} The map
1961	  \[
1962	    A_x \otimes_{R_x} B_x \;\longrightarrow\; (A \otimes^{p} B).\mathtt{stalk}\,x
1963	  \]
1964	  is built by the universal property of the tensor product over \(R_x\) from the
1965	  \(R_x\)-bilinear map of the two stalks
1966	  \[
1967	    (\mathrm{germ}_x a,\ \mathrm{germ}_x b) \;\longmapsto\;
1968	      \mathrm{germ}_x\bigl(a|_{U \cap V} \otimes b|_{U \cap V}\bigr),
1969	  \]
1970	  where \(a \in A(U)\) and \(b \in B(V)\) are chosen representatives; this is a
1971	  \emph{nested} colimit descent, descending first the \(A\)-variable out of the
1972	  filtered colimit \(\{A(U)\}_{U \ni x}\) and then the \(B\)-variable out of
1973	  \(\{B(V)\}_{V \ni x}\), passing to the common refinement \(U \cap V\) to form the
1974	  simple tensor and its germ. Well-definedness on germs (independence of the chosen
1975	  representatives and of the neighbourhoods \(U, V\)) is exactly filteredness of the
1976	  neighbourhood system.
1977	
1978	  It remains to check that the reverse map is \(R_x\)-bilinear, and in particular that
1979	  its underlying bilinear map is \emph{balanced} over \(R_x\), i.e. that pulling a scalar
1980	  out of the left variable agrees with pulling it out of the right. The key point is to
1981	  establish this balancing \emph{at the stalk level}, with every scalar living in the
1982	  stalk ring \(R_x = R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x\) throughout. Concretely, for a germ
1983	  \(\mathrm{germ}_x r\) of a section \(r \in R(W)\) one verifies the identity

1985	    \mathtt{revBihom}\bigl((\mathrm{germ}_x r)\cdot \mathrm{germ}_x a,\ \mathrm{germ}_x b\bigr)
1986	      \;=\; (\mathrm{germ}_x r)\cdot \mathtt{revBihom}\bigl(\mathrm{germ}_x a,\ \mathrm{germ}_x b\bigr),
1987	  \]

1996	  \(A_x \otimes_{R_x} B_x\). Because the whole computation takes place over the single
1997	  ring \(R_x\), no carrier identification intervenes and the balancing follows directly.

1999	  One must \emph{not} instead reduce this balancing to a section-level identity. If the
2000	  scalar were pushed through the presheaf restriction to a common neighbourhood \(W\) and

2006	  \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)-vs-\(\mathtt{RingCat}\) carrier-duality obstacle already met in

2564	\section{Pullback-monoidality: invertibility under a general scheme morphism}
2565	\label{sec:tensorobj_pullback_monoidality}
2566	
2567	The relative Picard consumer (\cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor}) re-bases its
2568	line-bundle carrier onto the \(\otimes\)-invertibility predicate
2569	\(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\) of \cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}, and its
2570	functorial structure maps are \emph{pullback} maps of \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules: the
2571	projection pullback \(\pi_T^*\) along \(C \times_S T \to T\) and the base-change
2572	pullback along \(C \times_S T' \to C \times_S T\). For these maps to descend to the
2573	\(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\) carrier one needs that \emph{pullback preserves
2574	\(\otimes\)-invertibility} for \emph{general} morphisms of schemes --- the
2575	projection and the base-change morphism are neither open immersions nor flat.
2576	
2577	This is a genuinely different fact from the open-immersion compatibility
2578	\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}, and not a corollary of it. That lemma handles
2579	restriction along an open immersion, and it does so by routing through the
2580	\emph{lax right} adjoint \(\mathtt{pushforward}/\mathtt{restrictScalars}\), which is
2581	strong monoidal there \emph{only} because an open immersion's structure-sheaf ring
2582	map is a local \emph{isomorphism} (\(f.\mathtt{appIso}\)). A general scheme
2583	morphism has no such isomorphism, so the right-adjoint route is unavailable.
2584	
2585	The general case is the \emph{left} adjoint, pullback \(f^* = \) inverse image
2586	\(=(\mathtt{pushforward}\,f).\mathtt{leftAdjoint}\). Conceptually pullback is
2587	``extension of scalars'', and over a single ring extension of scalars is indeed
2588	strong monoidal. But the sheaf-of-modules pullback is defined \emph{abstractly} as
2589	a left adjoint: it has \emph{no} sectionwise value formula and \emph{no} stalkwise
2590	value formula, so one cannot literally attach a sectionwise \(\mathtt{extendScalars}\)
2591	tensorator to \((f^*).\mathrm{obj}\). Moreover the doctrinal transfer that Mathlib
2592	ships, \(\mathtt{Adjunction.rightAdjointLaxMonoidal}\), produces a \emph{lax}
2593	structure on the \emph{right} adjoint from an oplax structure on the left; the
2594	oplax comparison maps on the left adjoint itself are \emph{not} provided for free.
2595	Consequently the monoidality of \(f^*\) is established here by \emph{local-chart
2596	finality}, not by a global sectionwise tensorator: one exhibits the comparison maps
2597	\(f^*(M \otimes N) \to f^*M \otimes f^*N\) and \(f^*\mathcal{O}_X \to \mathcal{O}_Y\)
2598	and proves each is an isomorphism by restricting to affine charts on which the
2599	comparison functor is \(\mathtt{Final}\) --- precisely the charts where the pullback
2600	\emph{is} locally extension of scalars --- and then globalizing ``iso on every chart
2601	\(\Rightarrow\) global iso''. This is the whole reason the general lemma is a separate
2602	result, with its own \emph{two}-phase proof (the cheap unit comparison and the
2603	genuinely new tensor comparison).
2604	
2605	It is worth noting why the monoidality route is the primary one rather than a
2606	detour. Restricting to \emph{flat} structure maps does \emph{not} help: flatness
2607	governs the exactness of \(f^*\), not whether the tensor comparison
2608	\(f^*(M \otimes N) \to f^*M \otimes f^*N\) is an isomorphism (that comparison is an
2609	isomorphism for \emph{every} morphism, flat or not), so a flat restriction would
2610	simplify nothing here. The only genuinely simpler-looking alternative --- routing
2611	through local triviality, ``invertible \(\Rightarrow\) locally \(\mathcal{O}\)
2612	\(\Rightarrow\) pulls back to locally \(\mathcal{O}\)'' --- is a mirage on this
2613	carrier: recovering \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\) from local triviality is the reverse
2614	bridge \(\mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial} \Rightarrow \mathtt{IsInvertible}\), i.e.\ exactly
2615	the dual/tensor-inverse manufacture that the invertibility-carrier pivot
2616	(\cref{sec:tensorobj_pic_carrier}) was built to delete. The pullback-monoidality
2617	route is therefore primary precisely because it pulls back the existing tensor-inverse
2618	witness directly, never reintroducing the deleted dual.
2619	
2620	\begin{lemma}
2621	[Pullback commutes with $\otimes$ for a general scheme morphism]
2622	  \label{lem:pullback_tensor_iso}
2623	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.pullbackTensorIso}
2624	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
2625	  Let \(f : Y \to X\) be a morphism of schemes and let
2626	  \(M, N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) be \emph{arbitrary} \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules.
2627	  Writing \(f^* = \mathtt{Scheme.Modules.pullback}\,f\) for the inverse-image functor
2628	  of \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules, the canonical comparison
2629	  \[
2630	    f^*(M \otimes_X N)
2631	      \;\xrightarrow{\;\sim\;}\;
2632	    (f^*M) \otimes_Y (f^*N)
2633	  \]
2634	  is an isomorphism, natural in \(M\) and \(N\). No local-freeness, flatness, or
2635	  open-immersion hypothesis is required: it holds for all \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
2636	  and all morphisms \(f\).
2637	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Modules on Ringed Spaces, Lemma
2638	  % \ref{lemma-tensor-product-pullback} (pullback of $\mathcal{O}$-modules along a
2639	  % morphism of ringed spaces commutes with tensor product, functorially)
2640	  % (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L2392-2400).
2641	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
2642	  % "\begin{lemma}
2643	  % \label{lemma-tensor-product-pullback}
2644	  % Let $f : (X, \mathcal{O}_X) \to (Y, \mathcal{O}_Y)$ be
2645	  % a morphism of ringed spaces. Let $\mathcal{F}$, $\mathcal{G}$
2646	  % be $\mathcal{O}_Y$-modules. Then
2647	  % $f^*(\mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_Y} \mathcal{G})
2648	  % = f^*\mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} f^*\mathcal{G}$
2649	  % functorially in $\mathcal{F}$, $\mathcal{G}$.
2650	  % \end{lemma}"
2651	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Modules on Ringed Spaces, Lemma
2652	  \texttt{lemma-tensor-product-pullback}: for a morphism of ringed spaces
2653	  \(f : (X, \mathcal{O}_X) \to (Y, \mathcal{O}_Y)\) and \(\mathcal{O}_Y\)-modules
2654	  \(\mathcal{F}, \mathcal{G}\), one has \(f^*(\mathcal{F} \otimes \mathcal{G}) =
2655	  f^*\mathcal{F} \otimes f^*\mathcal{G}\) functorially.}
2656	\end{lemma}

2658	\begin{proof}
2659	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:pullback_unit_iso}
2660	  Because the abstract left-adjoint pullback has no sectionwise value, the
2661	  comparison map must first be \emph{constructed} and then shown to be an isomorphism
2662	  by reduction to local charts. The argument has three parts: (a) build the
2663	  comparison map, (b) prove it is an isomorphism by a finality chart-chase, (c) observe
2664	  that this pointwise isomorphism is all the invertibility consumer needs (the full
2665	  strong-monoidal packaging is off the critical path).
2666	
2667	  \emph{(a) The tensor comparison map.} In parallel to the canonical unit comparison
2668	  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.pullbackObjUnitToUnit}\) of \cref{lem:pullback_unit_iso},
2669	  one constructs a tensor comparison
2670	  \[
2671	    \mathtt{pullbackObjTensorToTensor} :
2672	      f^*(M \otimes_X N)
2673	      \;\longrightarrow\;
2674	      (f^*M) \otimes_Y (f^*N),
2675	  \]
2676	  the oplax monoidal comparison \(\delta\) of \(f^*\) on the pair \((M, N)\). This map
2677	  is not available off the shelf --- the Mathlib pullback ships only the composition
2678	  coherences \(\mathtt{pullbackComp}\), \(\mathtt{pullbackId}\),
2679	  \(\mathtt{pullback\_assoc}\), and \emph{no} tensorator. It is produced from the
2680	  monoidal structure of the \emph{presheaf}-level pullback
2681	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\,\varphi\) (where, unlike the sheaf level, the
2682	  ambient \(\mathtt{MonoidalCategory}\) on presheaves of modules over a
2683	  \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)-valued ring presheaf \emph{does} exist), moved across the
2684	  sheafification using the genuine Mathlib lemma
2685	  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.sheafificationCompPullback}\) --- general (any morphism of
2686	  ring-sheaves) and already consumed in \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} --- which
2687	  commutes \(f^*\) with sheafification. The reconciliation of the right-hand side
2688	  after this move is supplied by the already-established sheafification-monoidality
2689	  brick (``sheafification is monoidal up to the unit'', cf.
2690	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}), which the eventual comparison consumes.
2691	
2692	  \emph{(b) It is an isomorphism, by local-chart finality.} This is the same
2693	  chart-chase that proves the unit comparison in \cref{lem:pullback_unit_iso}, run one
2694	  level up. Cover \(Y\) by affine charts; on such a chart \(V \subseteq Y\) factor the
2695	  inclusion as \(V.\iota \mathbin{;} f = g \mathbin{;} U.\iota\) through
2696	  \(g = f.\mathtt{resLE}\,U\,V\), whose comparison functor
2697	  \(\mathtt{Opens.map}\,g.\mathtt{base}\) is \(\mathtt{Final}\) (via
2698	  \(\mathtt{final\_of\_representablyFlat}\)). On a \(\mathtt{Final}\) chart the
2699	  pullback \emph{is} locally extension of scalars, which is \emph{strong monoidal} in
2700	  \(\mathtt{ModuleCat}\): the local comparison is exactly the \(\mathtt{extendScalars}\)
2701	  tensorator \((M \otimes_R N) \otimes_R S \cong (M \otimes_R S) \otimes_S
2702	  (N \otimes_R S)\), an isomorphism for every ring map with no hypothesis. The
2703	  restricted global \(\mathtt{pullbackObjTensorToTensor}\) is identified with this
2704	  local comparison through the naturality of the
2705	  \(\mathtt{pullbackComp}\)/\(\mathtt{restrictFunctorIsoPullback}\)/\(\mathtt{pullbackCongr}\)
2706	  cluster --- the same glue wired in \(\mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial.pullback}\). Finally
2707	  ``iso on every chart \(\Rightarrow\) global iso'' is the project's
2708	  \(\mathtt{isIso\_of\_isIso\_restrict}\) (\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}'s
2709	  globalizer). The residual work is the naturality lemma tying the restricted global
2710	  comparison to the local one.
2711	
2712	  \emph{(c) The pointwise isomorphism is all that is needed.} The sole consumer of
2713	  this lemma on the critical path, \cref{lem:isinvertible_pullback}, requires only the
2714	  comparison \emph{isomorphism at the single witness pair} \((M, N)\) together with its
2715	  naturality --- \emph{not} a strong-monoidal functor structure on \(f^*\). Parts
2716	  (a)--(b) already deliver exactly that: the comparison
2717	  \(\mathtt{pullbackObjTensorToTensor}\) at \((M, N)\), shown to be an isomorphism by the
2718	  finality chart-chase. We therefore take the asserted isomorphism
2719	  \(f^*(M \otimes_X N) \cong (f^*M) \otimes_Y (f^*N)\) to be \(\mathtt{asIso}\) of that
2720	  pointwise comparison, with naturality inherited from the naturality of the comparison
2721	  and of sheafification.
2722	
2723	  Packaging the whole family \(\{\delta_{M,N}\}\) into a strong-monoidal structure on
2724	  \(f^*\) via the Mathlib wrapper \(\mathtt{Functor.CoreMonoidal.ofOplaxMonoidal}\) (from
2725	  \([\mathtt{IsIso}\,\eta]\) and \([\forall X Y,\ \mathtt{IsIso}\,(\delta_{X,Y})]\)) is
2726	  possible, but it additionally requires a \emph{hand-built} \(\mathtt{OplaxMonoidal}\,f^*\)
2727	  instance --- there is no \(\mathtt{leftAdjointOplaxMonoidal}\) transfer (Mathlib ships
2728	  only \(\mathtt{rightAdjointLaxMonoidal}\), the opposite direction), so the oplax
2729	  structure with all its coherence is not free. That packaging is \emph{not} needed for
2730	  the invertibility consumer and is deferred as off the critical path; the pointwise
2731	  isomorphism above is the deliverable.
2732	\end{proof}

2852	\section{The invertibility-carrier Picard group}
2853	\label{sec:tensorobj_pic_carrier}
2854	
2855	The group law of \cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup} is carried on the
2856	\emph{locally-trivial} predicate \(\mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial}\), and on that carrier
2857	the existence of a tensor inverse for each class is an extra obligation
2858	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible}): one must \emph{manufacture} the dual
2859	object \(L^{-1} = \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_X}(L, \mathcal{O}_X)\) and prove the
2860	contraction \(L \otimes_X L^{-1} \cong \mathcal{O}_X\), which is the whole content of
2861	the dual-infrastructure build of \cref{sec:tensorobj_dual_infra}.
2862	
2863	This section carries the group law on a \emph{different} predicate for which that
2864	obligation evaporates: the \(\otimes\)-invertibility predicate
2865	\(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\) of \cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible},
2866	\[
2867	  \mathtt{IsInvertible}(M)
2868	  \;:\equiv\;
2869	  \exists\, N,\ \mathrm{Nonempty}\bigl(M \otimes_X N \cong \mathcal{O}_X\bigr).
2870	\]
2871	On this carrier the inverse of a class \([M]\) is supplied by the \emph{membership
2872	witness itself} --- the very \(N\) whose existence \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}(M)\)
2873	asserts --- so no inverse object need be constructed and no separate
2874	tensor-inverse lemma is consumed. The cost is shifted to a routine
2875	well-definedness fact (\cref{lem:isinvertible_inverse_welldef}: the witness \(N\) is
2876	determined up to isomorphism), proved from the monoidal coherence isomorphisms
2877	alone. The carrier is the scheme-theoretic analogue of Mathlib's
2878	\(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\), whose elements are exactly the iso-classes of invertible
2879	modules (Stacks tag 01CX).

30	% refinement is necessary because, by Kleiman \S 2, \(\Pic_X\) is never a Zariski
31	% (let alone \'etale) sheaf; the smallest \'etale sheaf below \(\Pic_X\) is the
32	% one that captures line bundles that are trivializable \emph{\'etale-locally},
33	% which is the right notion for Grothendieck--Mumford--Kleiman representability.
34	
35	\section{Setup and motivation}
36	\label{sec:relpic_setup}
37	
38	Fix a base field \(k\) and a smooth proper geometrically integral curve \(C\) over
39	\(k\). As in \cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}, write
40	\(\pi_T : C \times_k T \to T\) for the projection of a relative curve over a
41	\(k\)-scheme \(T\) and
42	\(\pi_T^* : \Pic(T) \to \Pic(C \times_k T)\) for the induced pullback of
43	isomorphism classes of line bundles (\cref{def:pullback_along_projection}).
44	
45	The set-valued relative Picard presheaf
46	\[
47	  \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) \;\;=\;\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \, / \, \pi_T^*\Pic(T)
48	\]
49	of \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined} was set up forgetting the
50	abelian-group structure inherited from tensor product. This chapter restores
51	that structure and then sheafifies. The first half (\S~REF
52	and \S~REF) verifies that the quotient is canonically
53	an abelian group and the functoriality of \cref{thm:pullback_natural} respects
54	that group structure; the result is a group-valued presheaf
55	\(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k} : (\Sch/k)^{op} \to \Ab\) on the category of \(k\)-schemes. The
56	second half (\S~REF) takes the \'etale sheafification
57	\(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et} := (\Pic^\sharp_{C/k})^\sim\) in the \'etale topology on
58	\((\Sch/k)^{op}\), and verifies that sheafification preserves the abelian-group
59	target. The \'etale-sheafified functor is the object represented by the Picard
60	scheme in \cref{chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability}.
61	
62	\section{Group structure on the relative Picard quotient}
63	\label{sec:relpic_group}
64	
65	The Picard group \(\Pic(X)\) of any scheme is canonically an abelian group under
66	tensor product of line bundles (Stacks tag 01CR; the inverse of \([\mathcal{L}]\)
67	is $[\mathcal{L}^{-1}] = [\mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\mathcal{L},
68	\mathcal{O}_X)]$). The pullback map
69	\(\pi_T^* : \Pic(T) \to \Pic(C \times_k T)\) of
70	\cref{def:pullback_along_projection} respects this structure: pullback of
71	\(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules sends \(\mathcal{O}_T\) to \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\)
72	and is multiplicative on tensor products.
73

76	  \label{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}
77	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp.addCommGroup}

92	  %     \(\)\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).\(\)"

98	  \(k\)-scheme. The Picard group \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) is an abelian group under
99	  tensor product of line bundles, and the pullback map
100	  \[

1	\chapter{Higher direct images $R^i f_*$ of quasi-coherent sheaves ($i \geq 1$)}
2	\label{chap:Cohomology_HigherDirectImage}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/HigherDirectImage.lean
4	% SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Tags 02KE
5	% (Quasi-coherence of higher direct images), 02KG (relative affine vanishing),
6	% and 02KH (Flat base change) (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex and
7	% references/stacks-coherent.md).
8	
9	\section{Setup and motivation}
10	
11	This chapter treats the higher derived direct images \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) for
12	\(i \geq 1\), complementing the \(i = 0\) (direct-image) case of
13	Chapter~\ref{chap:Cohomology_FlatBaseChange}. These higher direct images are the
14	single deepest foundation of the cohomology-and-base-change package: the
15	Cohen--Macaulay regularity bound, semicontinuity of fibre cohomology, and the
16	\(m\)-regularity estimate of FGA all rest on the basic finiteness and base-change
17	properties of \(R^i f_*\) recorded here.
18	
19	We use the classical cohomology-sheaf formulation (not the derived category) and a
20	{\v C}ech-complex approach, parallel to the \(i = 0\) treatment of
21	Chapter~\ref{chap:Cohomology_FlatBaseChange}.
22	
23	Throughout, \(f : X \to S\) is a morphism of schemes that is quasi-compact and
24	quasi-separated, and \(\mathcal{F}\) is a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module.
25	A base change of \(f\) along a morphism \(g : S' \to S\) is recorded by the
26	cartesian square obtained from the fibre product \(X' = X \times_S S'\):
27	\[
28	  \begin{array}{ccc}
29	    X' & \xrightarrow{\;g'\;} & X \\[2pt]
30	    {\scriptstyle f'}\big\downarrow & & \big\downarrow{\scriptstyle f} \\[2pt]
31	    S' & \xrightarrow{\;g\;} & S
32	  \end{array}
33	\]
34	Here \(f' : X' \to S'\) is the base change of \(f\), \(g' : X' \to X\) is the first
35	projection, and \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\) is the pullback of
36	\(\mathcal{F}\) to \(X'\).
37	
38	\section{Definition of the higher direct images}
39	
40	\begin{definition}\leanok
41	[Higher direct image]
42	  \label{def:higher_direct_image}
43	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.higherDirectImage}
44	  % NOTE (iter-233): the Lean `higherDirectImage` carries an explicit instance
45	  % hypothesis `[HasInjectiveResolutions X.Modules]`, because Mathlib has
46	  % `Abelian X.Modules` but NOT `EnoughInjectives` / `IsGrothendieckAbelian` for
47	  % `SheafOfModules` over a sheaf of rings (the Grothendieck-abelian instance is
48	  % only for `Sheaf J A` with a fixed value category). The definition is therefore
49	  % conditional; the hypothesis can be dropped once a lane proves that instance.
50	  % The file is not yet imported by the aggregator (orphan module).
51	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma
52	  % `lemma-relative-affine-vanishing' (proof), describing $R^i f_*$ as the
53	  % $i$-th right derived functor of $f_*$ via the sheafified presheaf
54	  % $V \mapsto H^i(f^{-1}(V), \mathcal{F}|_{f^{-1}(V)})$
55	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L188--192).
56	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "According to
57	  % Cohomology, Lemma \ref{cohomology-lemma-describe-higher-direct-images}
58	  % the sheaf
59	  % $R^if_*\mathcal{F}$ is the sheaf associated to the presheaf
60	  % $V \mapsto H^i(f^{-1}(V), \mathcal{F}|_{f^{-1}(V)})$."
61	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, \S\,Quasi-coherence of
62	  higher direct images (and the underlying Cohomology chapter).}
63	  Let \(f : X \to S\) be a morphism of schemes and let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a
64	  quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module. For \(i \geq 0\) the \emph{\(i\)-th
65	  higher direct image} \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) is the \(i\)-th right derived functor
66	  of the pushforward functor \(f_*\) applied to \(\mathcal{F}\). Concretely,
67	  \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) is the \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-module obtained as the
68	  sheafification of the presheaf
69	  \[
70	    V \;\longmapsto\; H^i\bigl(f^{-1}(V),\, \mathcal{F}|_{f^{-1}(V)}\bigr),
71	    \qquad V \subset S \text{ open},
72	  \]
73	  where \(H^i(-, -)\) denotes sheaf cohomology. For \(i = 0\) this recovers the
74	  ordinary pushforward \(R^0 f_*\mathcal{F} = f_*\mathcal{F}\).
75	\end{definition}
76	
77	\section{Quasi-coherence of the higher direct images}
78	
79	\begin{lemma}\leanok
80	[Quasi-coherence of higher direct images]
81	  \label{lem:higher_direct_image_quasi_coherent}
82	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.higherDirectImage_isQuasiCoherent}
83	  \uses{def:higher_direct_image}
84	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Tag 02KE (Lemma
85	  % `lemma-quasi-coherence-higher-direct-images', part (1))
86	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L741--757).
87	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $f : X \to S$ be a morphism of schemes.
88	  % Assume that $f$ is quasi-separated and quasi-compact.
89	  % \begin{enumerate}
90	  % \item For any quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-module $\mathcal{F}$ the
91	  % higher direct images $R^pf_*\mathcal{F}$ are quasi-coherent on $S$.
92	  % \item If $S$ is quasi-compact, there exists an integer $n = n(X, S, f)$
93	  % such that $R^pf_*\mathcal{F} = 0$ for all $p \geq n$ and any
94	  % quasi-coherent sheaf $\mathcal{F}$ on $X$.
95	  % \item In fact, if $S$ is quasi-compact we can find $n = n(X, S, f)$
96	  % such that for every
97	  % morphism of schemes $S' \to S$ we have $R^p(f')_*\mathcal{F}' = 0$
98	  % for $p \geq n$ and any quasi-coherent sheaf $\mathcal{F}'$
99	  % on $X'$. Here $f' : X' = S' \times_S X \to S'$ is the base change of $f$.
100	  % \end{enumerate}"

=== Jacobian.tex ===
\chapter{The Jacobian as an abelian variety}
\label{chap:Jacobian}
Let \(C\) be a smooth proper curve over \(k\) in the sense of Chapter~\ref{chap:Genus}, with genus \(g = g(C)\).
The Jacobian is built as the Albanese object of \(C\) in the category of smooth proper group schemes over \(k\), and the construction stratifies by genus. For positive genus the witness \emph{object} is the abelian variety \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) produced by \textbf{Route~A (the Picard scheme via FGA representability)}: this is the project's committed critical path, and it is \emph{mandatory} for the positive-genus object regardless of any other route choice --- the object must be a genuine \(g\)-dimensional abelian variety even when \(C(k) = \emptyset\) (see \cref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness} Route~A and \cref{def:positiveGenusWitness}). The FGA representability for \(\Pic_{C/k}\) and its Hilbert/Quot prerequisites are the single largest Mathlib build-out the project gates on. For genus \(0\) the witness object is instead the trivial scheme \(\Spec k\), \textbf{decoupled} from Route~A's representability engine; the only remaining content in that arm is a rigidity \emph{statement}, the characteristic-free keystone \cref{prop:rigidity_genus0_curve_to_AV} developed in \cref{chap:AbelianVarietyRigidity} via the Milne \S I.3 rigidity chain (Rigidity Lemma \(+\) additivity Corollary~1.5 \(+\) Corollary~1.2 \(+\) rational-map extension), \emph{cube-free} and \emph{not} the differential / Serre-duality argument. The over-\(\bar k\) declaration \cref{thm:rigidity_over_kbar} of \cref{chap:RigidityKbar}, which still carries \([\mathtt{CharZero}]\), is the retained fallback-(a) artifact and is \emph{not} the declaration the genus-\(0\) witness consumes.

117	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: Kleiman \S 2 does not supply a separate "proof" block
118	% for df:Pfs; the abelian-group structure is part of the definition (the word
119	% \emph{group} appears in df:aPf already, and df:Pfs is a quotient of the same
120	% abelian groups). The verbatim quotes for the statements above are therefore
121	% the same as for the lemma. The project-side proof is a structural restatement.
122	\begin{proof}
123	  
124	  The abelian-group structure on \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) under tensor product is
125	  standard (Stacks tag 01CR or Hartshorne II~\S 6): for line bundles
126	  \(\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{L}'\), the tensor product
127	  \(\mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}} \mathcal{L}'\) is again
128	  invertible, the structure sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\) is the unit, and
129	  the dual \(\mathcal{H}om(\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T})\) is the
130	  inverse. Pullback of \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules sends
131	  \(\mathcal{O}_T\) to \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\) (the structure sheaf is
132	  preserved by inverse image) and satisfies
133	  $\pi_T^*(\mathcal{N} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_T} \mathcal{N}') \cong
134	   \pi_T^*\mathcal{N} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}} \pi_T^*\mathcal{N}'$
135	  by the standard tensor compatibility of pullback. Therefore \(\pi_T^*\)
136	  preserves the unit, sends inverses to inverses, and is multiplicative on
137	  tensor products; it is a homomorphism of abelian groups.
138	
139	  The image \(H_T = \pi_T^*\Pic(T)\) is then a subgroup. The quotient set
140	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T) / H_T\) of
141	  \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined} carries the canonical quotient
142	  abelian-group structure (Lagrange's theorem on cosets specialised to an
143	  abelian quotient): for \(\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{L}' \in \Pic(C \times_k T)\), set
144	  \([\mathcal{L}] + [\mathcal{L}'] := [\mathcal{L} \otimes \mathcal{L}']\);
145	  well-definedness follows from the subgroup condition. The canonical map
146	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T) \to \Pic(C \times_k T) / H_T\) is by construction a
147	  surjective group homomorphism with kernel exactly \(H_T\).
148	\end{proof}
